title: Linux Systems
description: Linux from scratch (beginner rework in progress): 19 sub-topics from the shell through systemd, the kernel, containers, storage, networking, and embedded Linux. Beginner-first readings, code, and diagrams; tiered easy to hard.

lessons.title: What the Shell Is & Your First Commands
lessons.content:
Imagine controlling a computer not by clicking icons, but by typing instructions. That is the **shell**: a text-based way to tell a computer what to do. You type a **command** (an instruction), press Enter, the computer runs it, and prints the result back as text. It feels old-fashioned, but it is faster and far more powerful than clicking for many tasks, and it is how people control servers, Raspberry Pis, and machines they can only reach over the internet.

The window where you type is the **terminal**. The program inside it that actually reads and runs your commands is the shell; the most common one is called **bash**. (You may also hear of *zsh* or *sh* — same idea, slightly different flavors.)

A command usually has three parts, separated by spaces: the command itself, optional **options** (also called flags, like `-l`) that change how it behaves, and **arguments** that say what to act on.

```bash
ls -l /home/you
```

Here `ls` is the command (list files), `-l` is an option (use the long, detailed format), and `/home/you` is the argument (which folder to list).

Files live in **folders** (also called directories), and folders nest inside other folders, forming a **tree**. At the very top is `/`, called the **root**. A **path** like `/home/you` traces a route from the root down to a specific place. Here are the everyday commands for moving around and managing files:

```bash
pwd            # print working directory: where am I right now?
ls             # list the files here
cd /home/you   # change directory: move into a folder
cat notes.txt  # show the contents of a file
mkdir photos   # make a new folder
cp a.txt b.txt # copy a file
mv a.txt c.txt # move or rename a file
rm old.txt     # remove (delete) a file — careful, no trash bin!
```

Recap: the shell runs typed commands; a command is *command + options + arguments*; files live in a tree of folders rooted at `/`; and a handful of commands (`pwd`, `ls`, `cd`, `cat`, `mkdir`, `cp`, `mv`, `rm`) cover most everyday file work.

items.prompt: What is a "shell" in the simplest terms?
choices: A protective case for the computer's hardware
A program that reads commands you type and runs them, printing the results as text
A type of file that stores your photos
The physical keyboard you type on
items.explanation: The shell is the program that takes the text commands you type, runs them, and shows the output. It is a text-based alternative to clicking icons. The terminal is the window; the shell is the program inside it doing the work.

items.prompt: In the command `ls -l /home/you`, what is the `-l` part called?
choices: The command
An option (flag) that changes how the command behaves
The argument
A file path
items.explanation: `-l` is an option (also called a flag). It modifies how `ls` works — here, showing a detailed long listing. `ls` is the command, and `/home/you` is the argument naming what to act on.

items.prompt: Which command shows you which folder you are currently in?
choices: cd
ls
pwd
cat
items.explanation: `pwd` means "print working directory" — it tells you your current location. `cd` changes location, `ls` lists what is in the current folder, and `cat` shows a file's contents.

items.prompt: What does `cd /home/you` do?
choices: Creates a new folder called /home/you
Deletes the folder /home/you
Moves you into the folder /home/you so it becomes your current location
Copies /home/you somewhere else
items.explanation: `cd` means "change directory": it moves you into the named folder. To create a folder you would use `mkdir`, and to delete you would use `rm`.

items.prompt: The filesystem is often described as a "tree." What sits at the very top?
choices: The /home folder
The root, written as /
The folder you happen to be in
A file called tree.txt
items.explanation: The single top of the tree is the root, written as `/`. Every folder and file branches off from it. A path like `/home/you` traces a route starting at the root.

items.prompt: You want to make a copy of `report.txt` named `backup.txt`. Which command does that?
choices: mv report.txt backup.txt
cp report.txt backup.txt
rm report.txt backup.txt
cat report.txt backup.txt
items.explanation: `cp` copies a file, leaving the original in place. `mv` would move/rename it (the original name would disappear), `rm` deletes, and `cat` displays contents.

items.prompt: Why should you be extra careful with `rm`?
choices: It only works if you are an administrator
It permanently deletes the file — there is no recycle bin to recover it from
It renames the file but keeps a hidden copy
It compresses the file to save space
items.explanation: `rm` removes a file immediately and permanently; unlike a desktop trash can, there is normally no easy undo. That is why beginners are urged to double-check before deleting.

items.prompt: What does the command `mkdir photos` do?
choices: Deletes a folder named photos
Lists the files inside photos
Creates a new folder named photos
Shows the contents of a file named photos
items.explanation: `mkdir` means "make directory" — it creates a new, empty folder. To list contents you would use `ls`, and to view a file you would use `cat`.

items.prompt: Why is the shell especially useful for controlling a server you can only reach over the internet?
choices: Servers have no screen or mouse, so typed text commands are the natural way to control them remotely
The shell makes the server run faster
Servers can only understand the bash language and nothing else
Clicking icons is impossible on any computer
items.explanation: Remote servers and small devices like a Raspberry Pi often have no monitor or mouse attached. A text-based shell works perfectly over a network connection, which is why the command line is the standard way to manage them.

lessons.title: Pipes, Redirection & Handy Tools
lessons.content:
Each shell command is small and does one job. The magic is connecting them. Think of an **assembly line**: each station does one task, then passes its result to the next station. In the shell, that connector is the **pipe**, written `|`. A pipe takes the text output of one command and feeds it as the input of the next.

```bash
ls | wc -l
```

Here `ls` lists files, and `|` hands that list to `wc -l`, which counts lines — so this prints how many files are in the folder. You can chain several:

```bash
cat names.txt | sort | uniq
```

This reads a file, sorts the lines alphabetically, then collapses adjacent duplicates. (`uniq` only removes duplicates that are *next to each other*, which is why we usually `sort` first.)

A few small tools you will reach for constantly:

```bash
grep error log.txt   # show only lines that contain "error"
sort                 # sort lines
uniq                 # drop adjacent duplicate lines
wc -l                # count lines
head -n 5            # show the first 5 lines
tail -n 5            # show the last 5 lines
find . -name '*.txt' # find files named *.txt under here
```

**Redirection** is the other half of the story. A pipe connects command to command; redirection connects a command to a **file**. `>` sends a command's output into a file (overwriting it), `>>` appends to the end of a file, and `<` feeds a file in as input.

```bash
ls > files.txt       # save the listing into files.txt (overwrite)
echo hi >> log.txt   # add a line to the end of log.txt
sort < names.txt     # feed names.txt into sort
```

Put it together — this finds error lines, counts them, and saves the number:

```bash
grep error log.txt | wc -l > error-count.txt
```

Recap: a pipe `|` chains commands like an assembly line (output → input); redirection `>` `>>` `<` connects commands to files; and small tools like `grep`, `sort`, `uniq`, `wc`, `head`, `tail`, and `find` become powerful when piped together.

items.prompt: What does the pipe symbol `|` do in `command1 | command2`?
choices: Runs command1 and command2 at completely separate times with no connection
Takes the text output of command1 and feeds it as the input to command2
Saves command1's output into a file named command2
Deletes command1 if command2 succeeds
items.explanation: A pipe wires the output of the command on its left into the input of the command on its right — like one assembly-line station passing its work to the next. This is the core idea that makes small commands powerful together.

items.prompt: What does the pipeline `ls | wc -l` print?
choices: The names of all files, one per line
The number of files in the current folder
The total size of all files in bytes
The contents of every file
items.explanation: `ls` lists the files (one per line), and `wc -l` counts lines. Piped together, you get a count of how many files are listed — a handy way to count items in a folder.

items.prompt: Why does `uniq` usually need `sort` before it, as in `sort names.txt | uniq`?
choices: uniq cannot read files directly
uniq only removes duplicate lines that are next to each other, so sorting groups identical lines together first
sort is required before every command
uniq sorts in reverse, so sort cancels it out
items.explanation: `uniq` only collapses *adjacent* duplicate lines. If identical lines are scattered through the file, uniq misses them. Sorting first brings all identical lines together so uniq can remove them.

items.prompt: What is the difference between `ls > files.txt` and `ls >> files.txt`?
choices: There is no difference
`>` overwrites files.txt with the new output; `>>` adds the output to the end of files.txt
`>` appends and `>>` overwrites
`>` saves to a file and `>>` prints to the screen
items.explanation: A single `>` replaces the file's contents entirely. A double `>>` appends, keeping what was already there and adding the new output at the end. Mixing them up is a common way to accidentally erase a file.

items.prompt: Which command shows only the lines of `log.txt` that contain the word "error"?
choices: sort error log.txt
grep error log.txt
wc error log.txt
head error log.txt
items.explanation: `grep` searches text and prints only the lines that match the word or pattern you give it. `sort` reorders lines, `wc` counts them, and `head` shows the first few — none of them filter by content.

items.prompt: What is the key difference between a pipe `|` and redirection `>`?
choices: A pipe connects one command's output to another command; `>` connects a command's output to a file
They do exactly the same thing
A pipe writes to files; `>` connects commands
A pipe only works with two commands; `>` works with any number of files
items.explanation: A pipe feeds output into another *command*. Redirection with `>` sends output into a *file* (and `<` feeds a file in as input). One links commands together; the other links a command to a file.

items.prompt: What does this pipeline output: `cat names.txt | sort | head -n 3` ?
choices: The last 3 lines of names.txt, unsorted
The first 3 lines of names.txt after sorting it alphabetically
The number 3
All lines of names.txt sorted in reverse
items.explanation: `cat` outputs the file, `sort` orders the lines alphabetically, and `head -n 3` keeps the first 3 of those sorted lines. So you get the 3 alphabetically-first names. `tail` would give the last lines instead of `head`.

items.prompt: Which command finds all files ending in `.txt` within the current folder and its subfolders?
choices: grep '*.txt'
find . -name '*.txt'
ls .txt
wc -l *.txt
items.explanation: `find . -name '*.txt'` searches starting from the current folder (`.`) downward for names matching `*.txt`. `grep` searches inside file contents, not for filenames, and `ls` only lists one folder level.

items.prompt: What does `grep error log.txt | wc -l` tell you?
choices: The text of the first error in log.txt
How many lines in log.txt contain the word "error"
Whether log.txt exists
The size of log.txt in bytes
items.explanation: `grep error log.txt` produces just the matching lines, and `wc -l` counts them. The result is the number of lines containing "error" — a quick way to count occurrences.

lessons.title: Quoting, Wildcards & Brace Expansion
lessons.content:
Before the shell runs your line, it rewrites parts of it. Understanding these rewrites lets you predict what will happen *before* you press Enter, instead of being surprised.

**Wildcards (globbing)** are shortcuts for matching filenames. The shell replaces them with the actual matching files:

- `*` matches any run of characters (including none)
- `?` matches exactly one character
- `[abc]` matches one character from the set

```bash
ls *.txt      # every file ending in .txt
ls file?.txt  # file1.txt, fileA.txt (one char where ? is)
ls [ab]*.txt  # .txt files starting with a or b
```

If you write `ls *.txt` and there are three matching files, the shell turns it into `ls a.txt b.txt c.txt` and *then* runs `ls`. The command never sees the `*`.

**Quoting** turns off this rewriting when you want a literal character. Wrap text in single quotes `'...'` to take everything literally, or double quotes `"..."` to take it literally but still allow variables (covered next lesson).

```bash
echo *.txt     # prints the matching filenames
echo '*.txt'   # prints the literal text: *.txt
```

The classic beginner trap is **spaces in filenames**. A space normally separates arguments, so an unquoted name with a space is read as two separate things:

```bash
rm my report.txt    # WRONG: tries to delete "my" AND "report.txt"
rm "my report.txt"  # RIGHT: one file, quoted
```

**Brace expansion** generates lists of text. It happens *before* globbing and does not depend on existing files — it is pure text generation:

```bash
mkdir project/{src,test,docs}  # makes three folders
touch file{1..3}.txt           # file1.txt file2.txt file3.txt
```

Recap: `*`, `?`, and `[...]` are wildcards the shell expands into matching filenames; quotes turn off that expansion to keep text literal; spaces in filenames must be quoted; and braces `{...}` generate text lists regardless of what files exist.

items.prompt: In `ls *.txt`, what does the `*` match?
choices: Exactly one character
Any run of characters, so it matches every filename ending in .txt
Only hidden files
Nothing — it is always taken literally
items.explanation: `*` is a wildcard matching any sequence of characters (including an empty one). So `*.txt` matches any filename ending in `.txt`. The shell expands it to the actual matching files before running the command.

items.prompt: A folder contains exactly: cat.txt, dog.txt, and bird.png. What does `ls *.txt` actually run as, after the shell expands it?
choices: ls *.txt (the * is passed to ls unchanged)
ls cat.txt dog.txt
ls cat.txt dog.txt bird.png
ls *.png
items.explanation: The shell expands `*.txt` into the matching filenames before running the command, so `ls` actually receives `cat.txt dog.txt`. bird.png does not end in .txt, so it is excluded. The command never sees the literal `*`.

items.prompt: What does the `?` wildcard match?
choices: Any number of characters
Exactly one character
Only digits
A literal question mark only
items.explanation: `?` matches exactly one character of any kind. So `file?.txt` matches file1.txt and fileA.txt but not file.txt (zero chars) or file12.txt (two chars). `*` is the one that matches any number of characters.

items.prompt: What does `echo '*.txt'` print (note the single quotes)?
choices: The names of all .txt files in the folder
The literal text: *.txt
An error, because * cannot be quoted
Nothing at all
items.explanation: Single quotes turn off wildcard expansion, so the `*` stays literal and `echo` simply prints `*.txt`. Without the quotes, the shell would expand it into matching filenames first.

items.prompt: You have a file literally named `my report.txt` (with a space). Which command correctly deletes it?
choices: rm my report.txt
rm "my report.txt"
rm my-report.txt
rm my,report.txt
items.explanation: Without quotes the shell splits on the space and `rm` sees two names: "my" and "report.txt". Quoting the whole name keeps it as a single argument so rm targets the one real file. This is the classic spaces-in-filenames gotcha.

items.prompt: Does `mkdir test/{a,b,c}` require folders a, b, and c to already exist?
choices: Yes, brace expansion only works on existing folders
No — brace expansion is pure text generation, so it produces test/a, test/b, test/c regardless of what exists
Yes, the files must exist but not the folders
It only works if exactly one of them exists
items.explanation: Brace expansion is textual: `{a,b,c}` simply generates the strings, independent of the filesystem. So the command becomes `mkdir test/a test/b test/c`. Wildcards like `*` are the ones that depend on existing files.

items.prompt: What does `touch file{1..3}.txt` create?
choices: A single file named file{1..3}.txt
Three files: file1.txt, file2.txt, file3.txt
Nothing, because the files do not exist yet
A folder named file1to3
items.explanation: The brace range `{1..3}` expands to 1 2 3, so the line becomes `touch file1.txt file2.txt file3.txt`, creating three empty files. Braces generate the list as text before anything runs.

items.prompt: What is the practical difference between single quotes `'...'` and no quotes around `*.txt`?
choices: No quotes keeps it literal; single quotes expand the wildcard
Single quotes keep `*.txt` literal; without quotes the shell expands it into matching filenames
There is no difference for wildcards
Both always cause an error
items.explanation: Single quotes protect the text from expansion, so `*.txt` stays as the literal characters. Unquoted, the shell performs globbing and replaces `*.txt` with the actual matching files. Knowing this lets you predict the result before pressing Enter.

items.prompt: In a folder with apple.txt, avocado.txt, and banana.txt, what does `ls [ab]*.txt` list?
choices: Only apple.txt
All three files
Only banana.txt
Only files literally named [ab].txt
items.explanation: `[ab]` matches a single character that is either a or b, and `*` matches the rest. apple.txt and avocado.txt start with a, banana.txt starts with b — so all three match. The brackets pick the first letter; the star covers everything after it.

lessons.title: Variables, Exit Codes & Getting Help
lessons.content:
Commands can remember things and react to success or failure. This lesson covers the pieces that make the shell feel programmable.

**Variables** are named boxes that hold text. You set one with `name=value` (no spaces around the `=`) and read it back with a `$` in front:

```bash
greeting=hello
echo $greeting     # prints: hello
```

Some variables are set up for you. The most important is **`$PATH`**: a colon-separated list of folders the shell searches to find a command. When you type `ls`, the shell looks through each folder in `$PATH` until it finds a program named `ls`. If none has it, you get "command not found".

```bash
echo $PATH   # e.g. /usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin
```

**Command substitution** lets you capture one command's output and drop it into another, using `$(...)`:

```bash
echo "Today is $(date)"   # runs date, inserts its output
now=$(date)               # stores the output in a variable
```

**Exit codes** tell you whether a command succeeded. After any command, the special variable **`$?`** holds its result: `0` means success, and any other number means failure.

```bash
ls /home
echo $?    # 0 if /home exists, non-zero if it failed
```

You can chain commands based on success. `&&` runs the next command only if the previous one succeeded; `||` runs the next one only if the previous one failed:

```bash
mkdir build && cd build      # cd only if mkdir worked
ping -c1 host || echo "down" # echo only if ping failed
```

**Getting help** is a command away. `man ls` opens the full manual for `ls`; `ls --help` prints a quick summary of options. When you forget what a command does, these are where to look.

Recap: variables store text and are read with `$`; `$PATH` is the search list for commands; `$(...)` captures a command's output; `$?` holds the last exit code (0 = success); `&&` and `||` chain on success/failure; and `man` and `--help` give you documentation.

items.prompt: After running `greeting=hello`, how do you print the value with echo?
choices: echo greeting
echo $greeting
echo =greeting
echo "greeting"
items.explanation: You read a variable's value by putting `$` in front of its name, so `echo $greeting` prints `hello`. Without the `$`, echo just prints the literal word "greeting".

items.prompt: Why might `greeting = hello` (with spaces around =) fail to create a variable?
choices: Variables cannot hold the word hello
Variable assignment must have no spaces around the = sign
You must use quotes around hello
Variables must start with a capital letter
items.explanation: In the shell, assignment is written `name=value` with no spaces. With spaces, the shell reads `greeting` as a command to run, not an assignment. This trips up many beginners.

items.prompt: What is `$PATH`?
choices: The name of the current folder
A colon-separated list of folders the shell searches to find commands you type
The path to your home directory only
A list of every file on the computer
items.explanation: `$PATH` lists the folders, separated by colons, where the shell looks for programs. When you type `ls`, it searches each folder in turn until it finds the `ls` program.

items.prompt: You type a command and get "command not found", even though you are sure it is installed. What is a likely cause?
choices: The command was deleted from your variables
The folder containing the command is not listed in $PATH, so the shell cannot find it
You used too many spaces
The terminal needs to be restarted to run any command
items.explanation: "command not found" usually means the program is not in any folder listed in `$PATH`. The shell only searches those folders, so a program installed elsewhere will not be found unless you give its full path or add its folder to PATH.

items.prompt: What does `echo "Today is $(date)"` print?
choices: The literal text: Today is $(date)
Today is, followed by the output of the date command
An error, because $(...) is invalid
Just the word date
items.explanation: `$(...)` is command substitution: the shell runs `date`, captures its output, and inserts it in place. So you get something like "Today is Wed Jun 18 ...". Double quotes still allow this substitution.

items.prompt: After a command runs, what does `$?` equal if the command succeeded?
choices: The command's full output
The number of files it touched
items.explanation: `$?` holds the exit code of the last command. A value of 0 means success; any non-zero value (like 1 or 2) signals some kind of failure. In shell-land, zero is the good news.

items.prompt: What does `mkdir build && cd build` do?
choices: Always runs both commands no matter what
Runs mkdir, and only runs cd if mkdir succeeded
Runs cd first, then mkdir
Runs cd only if mkdir failed
items.explanation: `&&` means "and then, only if the first succeeded." So `cd build` runs only if `mkdir build` worked. If the folder could not be created, the cd is skipped — which prevents moving into a folder that does not exist.

items.prompt: In `ping -c1 host || echo "down"`, when does `echo "down"` run?
choices: Always, after ping
Only if ping succeeds
Only if ping fails (returns a non-zero exit code)
Never, because || is invalid
items.explanation: `||` means "or else" — the right side runs only if the left side failed. So if ping cannot reach the host, the exit code is non-zero and `echo "down"` runs. If ping succeeds, the echo is skipped.

items.prompt: You want a quick reminder of the options for the `ls` command. Which is a good way to get help?
choices: ls --help  (or  man ls)
echo ls
ls $PATH
rm ls
items.explanation: `ls --help` prints a brief summary of options, and `man ls` opens the full manual page. These built-in help tools are the right place to look when you forget what a command or its options do.

lessons.title: One Big Tree of Folders
lessons.content:
**Hook.** On Windows you might keep files on the C: drive and a USB stick on D:. Linux does something simpler: there is just *one* big tree of folders, and everything lives somewhere inside it.

**Build.** That tree starts at a single folder called the **root**, written as a plain slash: `/`. ("Folder" and "directory" mean the same thing; Linux usually says *directory*.) Every other folder branches off from `/`. So `/home` is the folder named `home` that sits directly inside the root, and `/home/alice` is the folder `alice` inside `home`.

There are no drive letters. When you plug in a USB stick or add a second disk, Linux *mounts* it onto some folder in the tree, so its contents simply appear at a path like `/media/usb`. From your point of view it is all one tree.

Think of it like a building directory in a lobby: a single map that tells you which floor holds what, so you never wander. The root `/` is the lobby; the top-level folders are the floors.

```bash
ls /            # list the top-level folders inside the root
cd /home/alice  # move into alice's home folder
pwd             # "print working directory": shows where you are now
```

A path that starts with `/` is an **absolute path**: it spells out the route all the way from the root, so it means the same thing no matter where you currently are.

**Recap.** Linux organizes all files as one tree starting at `/` (the root). No drive letters; extra disks are mounted onto folders inside that one tree.

items.prompt: In Linux, what does a single forward slash `/` by itself refer to?
choices: The user's home folder
The root: the single top folder that the whole tree starts from
A network drive
The current folder you are in
items.explanation: `/` is the root, the one folder at the very top of the tree. Everything else lives somewhere inside it. The current folder is `.` and your home folder is written `~`.

items.prompt: How does Linux differ from Windows when it comes to disks like C: and D:?
choices: Linux uses drive letters too, just lowercase
Linux has no drive letters; every disk appears as a folder inside the one tree
Linux can only use one disk at a time
Linux hides all disks from the user
items.explanation: Linux has no drive letters. A second disk or USB stick is mounted onto a folder (a mount point), so its files just show up at a path like /media/usb inside the single tree.

items.prompt: What does the path `/home/alice` describe?
choices: A folder named alice, inside a folder named home, inside the root
Three separate disks
A file called home.alice
A command that creates a user
items.explanation: Reading left to right from the root: `/` then `home` then `alice`. Each slash separates one level of folder from the next, so this is the alice folder inside home inside the root.

items.prompt: You plug in a USB stick and its files appear at `/media/usb`. What happened?
choices: The stick was given the drive letter U:
The stick was mounted onto the /media/usb folder so its contents appear there in the tree
Linux copied the files into your home folder
The stick replaced the root folder
items.explanation: Mounting attaches a disk's contents onto a folder in the existing tree. After mounting at /media/usb, browsing that folder shows the stick's files. There is still just one tree.

items.prompt: Why is a path that starts with `/`, such as `/etc/hosts`, called an absolute path?
choices: Because it can never be deleted
Because it spells out the full route from the root, so it means the same thing wherever you are
Because it is the fastest kind of path
Because only the root user can read it
items.explanation: An absolute path begins at the root `/` and lists every step from there, so it is unambiguous no matter your current folder. A path that does NOT start with `/` is relative to wherever you currently are.

items.prompt: Which command tells you which folder you are currently sitting in?
choices: ls
cd
pwd
mkdir
items.explanation: `pwd` means "print working directory" and prints your current location as an absolute path. `ls` lists contents, `cd` moves you, and `mkdir` makes a new folder.

items.prompt: The lesson compares the filesystem to a building directory in a lobby. What is that analogy getting at?
choices: Files are heavy like a building
There is one shared map starting from a single point, so you always know where to look
Every folder needs a key
Linux is only for large companies
items.explanation: Like one lobby directory listing what is on each floor, the tree gives a single, predictable map starting at the root, so you know where things live instead of guessing.

items.prompt: What does `cd /home/alice` do?
choices: Deletes the alice folder
Copies alice's files to the root
Moves you into the /home/alice folder so it becomes your current location
Lists everything in alice's folder
items.explanation: `cd` means "change directory": it moves your current location to the folder you name. To see what is inside afterward you would run `ls`.

lessons.title: The Standard Map: Where Things Live
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Now that everything is one tree, you need to know which top-level folder holds what. Happily, Linux follows a *standard map* (its official name is the Filesystem Hierarchy Standard, or FHS) so the layout is the same across systems. Learn the map once and you will know where to look on any Linux machine.

**Build.** Here are the top-level folders you will meet most often:

- `/home` -- your personal files. Each user gets a folder here, like `/home/alice`.
- `/etc` -- system **configuration**: plain-text settings files for the whole machine. (Say it "et-see".)
- `/bin` and `/usr/bin` -- **programs** (the actual command files like `ls` and `cp`). "bin" is short for *binaries*, meaning runnable programs.
- `/usr` -- the bulk of installed software lives under here.
- `/var` -- data that **changes** over time, especially logs in `/var/log` (a diary of what the system has been doing).
- `/tmp` -- a scratch space for temporary files; usually wiped when the machine restarts.
- `/dev` -- your hardware **devices**, presented as files (more on that next lesson).
- `/boot` -- the files needed to start the computer.
- `/proc` -- not real files on a disk, but a live window into what the system is doing right now.

```bash
ls /etc       # peek at system config files
ls /var/log   # the system's log files (its diary)
which ls      # shows the program file behind a command, e.g. /usr/bin/ls
```

**Recap.** Config goes in `/etc`, your stuff in `/home`, programs in `/bin` and `/usr/bin`, logs in `/var/log`. When something breaks, the map tells you where to look.

items.prompt: Using the map above, where would you look for the system's log files?
choices: /etc/log
/var/log
/home/log
/boot/log
items.explanation: Logs are data that keeps changing as the system runs, so they live under /var (variable data), specifically /var/log. /etc holds configuration, not logs.

items.prompt: Which top-level folder holds system-wide configuration (settings) files?
choices: /var
/etc
/bin
/tmp
items.explanation: /etc is the home of system configuration: plain-text settings files for the machine. /bin holds programs, /var holds changing data like logs, and /tmp is scratch space.

items.prompt: Where do an ordinary user's personal files live?
choices: In /etc, under their username
In /home, in a folder named after them, like /home/alice
In /bin
In /proc
items.explanation: Each user gets a personal folder under /home, such as /home/alice. /etc is for system config, /bin for programs, and /proc is a live view of the running system.

items.prompt: The word "bin" in /bin and /usr/bin is short for what, and what do those folders hold?
choices: "bin" = backup; they hold backups
"bin" = binaries (runnable programs); they hold the actual command files like ls and cp
"bin" = bytes; they hold raw data
"bin" = bind; they hold network settings
items.explanation: "bin" stands for binaries, meaning runnable program files. When you type ls, the system runs the program file found in a bin folder such as /usr/bin/ls.

items.prompt: What is special about files placed in /tmp?
choices: They are encrypted automatically
They are scratch/temporary files and are usually cleared when the machine restarts
They can only be read by the root user
They are backed up every night
items.explanation: /tmp is a temporary scratch area; its contents are typically wiped on reboot. Do not keep anything important there. Long-lived data belongs in /home or /var.

items.prompt: Which folder holds the files the computer needs in order to start up?
choices: /start
/boot
/etc
/run
items.explanation: /boot contains the startup files, including the Linux kernel image and the bootloader's files, that get the machine going before the rest of the tree is available.

items.prompt: You run `which ls` and it prints `/usr/bin/ls`. What did that tell you?
choices: That ls is broken
The actual program file behind the ls command lives at /usr/bin/ls
That you have no permission to run ls
That ls is a folder
items.explanation: `which` reports the location of the program file that runs when you type a command. Here the ls command is the program file /usr/bin/ls, confirming programs live in bin folders.

items.prompt: A service is misbehaving. Based on the standard map, where do you look FIRST to see what it has been doing?
choices: /home, for the user's files
/var/log, where the system keeps its logs
/bin, where programs live
/tmp, the scratch space
items.explanation: Logs are the system's diary of events and errors, and they live in /var/log. Knowing the map turns "where do I even look?" into a quick, confident answer.

lessons.title: Everything Is a File
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Here is one of Linux's most surprising ideas: almost everything looks like a file, even your hardware. Your keyboard, your disk, even a stream of random numbers, all show up as file-like things you can open and read.

**Build.** A normal file is just a named bundle of data. Linux extends that idea: devices appear as special files under `/dev`. For example `/dev/null` is a "trash can" file that throws away anything written to it, and `/dev/zero` hands out endless zeros. The payoff is that the same simple tools, opening, reading, and writing, work on a text file, a disk, or a device. You do not need a different toolset for each.

Underneath a filename, Linux stores the real file as an **inode** (think: index node) -- a small record holding the file's details: who owns it, its permissions, its size, timestamps, and where the data sits. Interestingly, the *name* is not stored in the inode. A folder is really just a list that pairs names with inodes.

Because names and inodes are separate, you can give one file a second name. A **hard link** is a second name pointing at the very same inode. A **symbolic link** (or *symlink*) is different: it is a tiny signpost file that just stores a path to another file by name; if you delete the target, the signpost dangles and points at nothing.

```bash
ls -l /dev/null        # a device shown as a file
echo hi > note.txt
ln -s note.txt link    # symlink: a signpost pointing at note.txt
ls -l link             # shows  link -> note.txt
```

**Recap.** "Everything is a file" means devices use the same read/write tools as text. Names live in folders; the real file is an inode. A hard link is a second name; a symlink is a signpost storing a path.

items.prompt: What does the phrase "everything is a file" practically mean in Linux?
choices: Every file is the same size
Even devices like disks and keyboards can be accessed with the same open/read/write tools as ordinary files
You can only store text, never images
All files must live in one folder
items.explanation: Devices, and other resources, appear as file-like objects (mostly under /dev), so the same simple tools work everywhere. That uniformity is the whole point; it is not about file sizes.

items.prompt: Which folder is where hardware devices show up as special files?
choices: /etc
/dev
/var
/home
items.explanation: /dev (short for devices) is where Linux presents hardware as files. /etc holds config, /var holds changing data, and /home holds personal files.

items.prompt: What is `/dev/null` commonly used for?
choices: Storing important backups
A trash can: anything written to it is silently discarded
Listing all users
Holding the system clock
items.explanation: /dev/null is a special device file that throws away everything written to it, handy when you want to discard output you do not care about. It does not store anything.

items.prompt: What is an inode?
choices: The text inside a file
A small record holding a file's details (owner, permissions, size, where the data lives) -- but not its name
A type of network cable
The folder a file is stored in
items.explanation: An inode is the behind-the-scenes record of a file's metadata and where its data sits. Notably, the filename is NOT in the inode; names are kept in folders that map a name to an inode.

items.prompt: If a filename is not stored in the inode, where is it kept?
choices: In the file's data blocks
In a folder, which is really a list pairing names with inodes
In /etc/names
Nowhere; files have no names
items.explanation: A folder (directory) is essentially a table that pairs each name with an inode number. That separation of name from inode is exactly what makes links possible.

items.prompt: What is a hard link?
choices: A copy of the file's data in a new location
A second name that points at the very same inode as the original
A shortcut that stores the path to another file
A link that only root can follow
items.explanation: A hard link is just another name for the same inode, so both names refer to one identical file. (A symlink, by contrast, only stores a path to a name.)

items.prompt: You run `ln -s note.txt link`, then delete note.txt. What is the state of `link`?
choices: link still holds the original data
link is a dangling symlink: a signpost now pointing at a path that no longer exists
link is automatically deleted as well
link turns into a hard link
items.explanation: A symlink only stores the path "note.txt" by name. Delete the target and the signpost dangles, pointing at nothing. A hard link would have kept the data, since it shares the inode.

items.prompt: What is the key difference between a symbolic link and a hard link?
choices: A symlink stores a path to another name; a hard link is a second name for the same inode
There is no difference; they are two words for the same thing
A hard link can only point to folders
A symlink uses twice the disk space of the original file
items.explanation: A symlink is a signpost holding a path (so it can break if the target moves). A hard link shares the actual inode, so it is a true equal second name for the same file data.

lessons.title: Owners and Permissions: rwx for user, group, other
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Picture a shared document with sharing settings: some people can view, some can edit, others have no access. Linux gives every file the same kind of settings, and this is the foundation of Linux security.

**Build.** Every file has an **owner** (a user) and a **group** (a named set of users). Permissions are then set for three audiences: the **user** (owner), the **group**, and **other** (everyone else). Each audience gets three switches:

- **r** = read (look at the contents)
- **w** = write (change the contents)
- **x** = execute (run it, if it is a program)

Run `ls -l` and you will see a string like `-rwxr-xr--`. The first character is the type (`-` for a file, `d` for a directory). The next nine are three groups of three: owner, then group, then other. So `-rwxr-xr--` means: owner can read/write/run, group can read and run, everyone else can only read.

One classic gotcha: on a **folder**, `x` does not mean "run". It means "may enter / pass through this folder". Without `x` on a folder you cannot `cd` into it or reach anything inside, even if you can see its name.

```bash
ls -l file.txt        # shows the permission string and owner
chmod u+x script.sh   # give the owner permission to run it
chmod g-w file.txt    # take write away from the group
chown alice file.txt  # make alice the owner (admin only)
```

`chmod` (change mode) edits the r/w/x switches; `chown` (change owner) sets who owns the file.

**Recap.** Owner, group, other each get read/write/execute. Read the string left to right in groups of three. On a folder, `x` means "enter it", not "run it".

items.prompt: Decode the permission string `-rwxr-xr--`. Which statement is TRUE?
choices: It is a folder; the owner can only read it
It is a file: the owner can read/write/run, the group can read and run, and everyone else can only read
It is a file that everyone can edit
It is a file; the owner has no permissions at all
items.explanation: First char `-` means a regular file. Then in threes: owner `rwx` (read+write+run), group `r-x` (read+run, a dash means that switch is off), other `r--` (read only). Read it left to right in groups of three.

items.prompt: Permissions are set for three audiences. What are they?
choices: Admin, guest, and visitor
User (the owner), group, and other (everyone else)
Read, write, and execute
Local, remote, and network
items.explanation: The three audiences are the owning user, the file's group, and other (everyone else). Read/write/execute are the three permissions each audience can be granted, not the audiences themselves.

items.prompt: On an ordinary file, what do r, w, and x mean?
choices: read, wipe, extend
read (view contents), write (change contents), execute (run it as a program)
receive, wait, exit
remote, web, external
items.explanation: r = read the contents, w = write/change the contents, x = execute (run) the file as a program. A dash in place of any letter means that permission is switched off.

items.prompt: In an `ls -l` listing, the permission string begins with `d`, as in `drwxr-xr-x`. What does that leading `d` tell you?
choices: The file is dangerous
The item is a directory (folder), not a regular file
The owner is named 'd'
The file is disabled
items.explanation: The first character is the type: `-` for a regular file and `d` for a directory. The nine characters after it are the owner/group/other permissions.

items.prompt: What does the execute (x) permission mean on a FOLDER, as opposed to a file?
choices: It lets you run the folder as a program
It lets you enter (traverse into) the folder and reach things inside it
It lets you rename the folder
It has no effect on folders
items.explanation: This is the classic gotcha: on a folder, x means "may enter / pass through". Without x you cannot cd into the folder or reach files inside, even if you can read its listing.

items.prompt: Which command changes a file's read/write/execute permissions?
choices: chown
chmod
chgrp
ls -l
items.explanation: chmod ("change mode") edits the r/w/x switches. chown changes who owns the file, chgrp changes the group, and ls -l only displays the permissions.

items.prompt: What does `chown alice file.txt` do?
choices: Gives alice read permission only
Makes alice the owner of file.txt
Deletes file.txt if alice owns it
Runs file.txt as alice
items.explanation: chown ("change owner") sets who owns the file, here making alice the owner. Handing a file to another user normally requires administrator (root) privileges.

items.prompt: You wrote a program file and want only its owner to be allowed to run it. Which command adds that permission?
choices: chmod o+x script.sh
chmod u+x script.sh
chmod a-x script.sh
chown root script.sh
items.explanation: In symbolic form, u = user (owner), g = group, o = other, a = all. `u+x` adds execute for the owner only. `o+x` would add it for everyone else, and `a-x` would remove it from all.

items.prompt: Why are owners and permissions described as the foundation of Linux security?
choices: They make files load faster
They control who can read, change, or run each file, keeping users from interfering with each other's and the system's files
They encrypt every file by default
They hide files from the owner
items.explanation: Permissions decide who may read, write, or run a file. That is how Linux keeps one user out of another's files and protects system files, the everyday basis of its security.

lessons.title: Number Shortcuts and Special Bits
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Typing `chmod u+x,g+r,o+r` gets tedious. Linux offers a shorthand: a three-digit number that captures all nine permission switches at once. You will see numbers like `755` everywhere, so it helps to read them.

**Build.** The trick is to give each switch a value: **r = 4, w = 2, x = 1**. For one audience, add up the switches that are on. So `rwx` = 4+2+1 = 7, `r-x` = 4+0+1 = 5, `r--` = 4. Do this for owner, group, and other and you get three digits.

That is why `rwxr-xr-x` is **755**: owner 7, group 5, other 5. A common file setting is **644** (`rw-r--r--`): owner can read and write, everyone else read only. You apply it with `chmod 755 file` or `chmod 644 file`. This is just another way to write the same thing as the symbolic `u+x` style from the last lesson.

```bash
chmod 755 script.sh   # rwxr-xr-x  (owner full; others read+run)
chmod 644 notes.txt   # rw-r--r--  (owner read/write; others read)
```

**Special bits (light touch).** A few folders use extra bits beyond rwx. The most common is the **sticky bit** on `/tmp`: because everyone can write there, the sticky bit adds a rule that you may only delete *your own* files, not other people's. There are also **setuid** and **setgid** bits that let a program run with its owner's or group's powers; you will see these occasionally but rarely set them yourself as a beginner.

**Recap.** r=4, w=2, x=1; add per audience to get a digit; three digits set everything. 755 = rwxr-xr-x, 644 = rw-r--r--. Special bits like the sticky bit on /tmp add a few extra safety rules.

items.prompt: Using r=4, w=2, x=1, what three-digit number equals `rwxr-xr-x`?
items.explanation: Owner rwx = 4+2+1 = 7; group r-x = 4+0+1 = 5; other r-x = 4+0+1 = 5. So rwxr-xr-x is 755. 777 would be rwx for everyone; 644 is rw-r--r--.

items.prompt: In the number shortcut, what value does each permission have?
items.explanation: read = 4, write = 2, execute = 1. You add up the ones that are on for each audience. These specific values let every combination map to a unique digit from 0 to 7.

items.prompt: For one audience, the digit 7 means which permissions are on?
choices: Read only
Read and write, but not execute
Read, write, and execute (all three)
Execute only
items.explanation: 7 = 4 + 2 + 1 = read + write + execute, so all three switches are on. That is the rwx triad fully enabled.

items.prompt: What does `chmod 644 notes.txt` set the permissions to?
choices: rwxr--r--
rw-r--r--  (owner read/write; group and other read only)
rw-rw-rw-
items.explanation: Owner 6 = 4+2 = rw-; group 4 = r--; other 4 = r--. So 644 is rw-r--r--: the owner can read and write, everyone else can only read. A very common setting for documents.

items.prompt: What single digit represents the permissions `r-x`?
items.explanation: r = 4 and x = 1, and w is off (0), so 4 + 0 + 1 = 5. (6 would be rw-, and 7 would be rwx.)

items.prompt: How does the number form (like 755) relate to the symbolic form (like u+x) from the previous lesson?
choices: They control different things entirely
They are two ways of expressing the same read/write/execute permissions
The number form only works for folders
The symbolic form is more powerful and the number form is fake
items.explanation: Both set the same r/w/x switches. Numbers set all nine at once to an exact state; symbolic mode (u+x, g-w) nudges individual switches up or down. Use whichever is clearer for the task.

items.prompt: Why does the shared /tmp folder use the sticky bit?
choices: So files in /tmp run faster
So that even though everyone can write there, you may only delete your own files, not other people's
So /tmp is cleared on reboot
So only root can use /tmp
items.explanation: /tmp is writable by everyone, which would normally let anyone delete anyone's files. The sticky bit adds the rule that only a file's owner (or root) can delete it, protecting people's scratch files from each other.

items.prompt: At a beginner level, what is the rough idea of the setuid bit on a program?
choices: It makes the program invisible
It lets the program run with the powers of the program's owner, rather than the person running it
It deletes the program after one use
It doubles the program's speed
items.explanation: setuid lets a program run with its owner's privileges, so an ordinary user can perform a tightly controlled task that normally needs more power. You will see it occasionally; you rarely need to set it yourself early on.

items.prompt: Someone runs `chmod 777 secret.txt`. Why is that usually a bad idea?
choices: 777 is not a valid permission number
777 gives read, write, AND execute to everyone, so any user on the system can change the file
It makes the file unreadable
It deletes the file
items.explanation: 777 = rwx for owner, group, and other, meaning everyone can read, modify, and run it. That is wide open and rarely what you want for a file with private contents.

lessons.title: Beyond the Basics: ACLs and Capabilities
lessons.content:
**Hook.** The owner/group/other model is simple, but sometimes too simple. What if you want to share one file with exactly one extra person, who is not the owner and not in the group? For situations like that, Linux has a few extra tools. You will not need these every day, but it helps to know they exist.

**Build.** First, **ACLs** (Access Control Lists). The plain model names only one owner and one group. An ACL lets you add extra named people or groups with their own permissions, without disturbing the owner or group. You add an entry with `setfacl` and view them with `getfacl`. A clue that a file has ACLs: in `ls -l` you will see a small `+` at the end of its permission string.

```bash
setfacl -m u:bob:rw report.txt   # also let bob read and write
getfacl report.txt               # list all the extra permission entries
```

Second, **capabilities**. Traditionally, a program that needed one special power had to run as the all-powerful root user, which is risky: if it is tricked, an attacker gets *everything*. Capabilities split root's power into many small, separate powers, so a program can be granted just the one it needs (for example, the ability to send a network ping) and nothing more. This is the modern, safer alternative to giving a program full root powers. You inspect them with `getcap` and set them with `setcap`.

```bash
getcap /usr/bin/ping   # see which single power ping was granted
```

**Recap.** ACLs add extra named users/groups beyond owner/group/other (look for the `+` in `ls -l`). Capabilities hand a program one specific power instead of all of root, which is safer. Both are tools to reach for only when the basic model is not enough.

items.prompt: What can an ACL do that the basic owner/group/other model cannot?
choices: Encrypt the file
Grant permissions to extra named users or groups, beyond the single owner and group
Make the file run faster
Hide the file from the owner
items.explanation: The basic model names only one owner and one group. An ACL lets you add more named users or groups with their own permissions, perfect for sharing with one extra person without changing ownership.

items.prompt: In `ls -l`, a file's permission string ends with a `+`, like `-rw-r--r--+`. What does that signal?
choices: The file is being downloaded
An ACL is present, granting extra permissions beyond the basic ones shown
The file is read-only
The owner is the root user
items.explanation: A trailing `+` means the file has an ACL with extra entries beyond the standard nine bits. Use getfacl to see the full set of who-can-do-what.

items.prompt: You must give exactly one extra user, bob, read and write on report.txt, without changing its owner or group. Which command fits?
choices: chmod o+rw report.txt
chown bob report.txt
setfacl -m u:bob:rw report.txt
chmod 777 report.txt
items.explanation: setfacl -m u:bob:rw adds an ACL entry for bob alone. chmod o+rw would open the file to everyone, chown would hand the whole file to bob, and 777 opens it to all.

items.prompt: Which command shows all the ACL entries on a file?
choices: getfacl
getcap
chmod
ls -a
items.explanation: getfacl lists every ACL entry (the owner/group/other lines plus any extra named users or groups). getcap is for capabilities, and chmod changes basic permissions.

items.prompt: What problem do capabilities solve?
choices: They make files load faster
They split root's all-or-nothing power into small pieces, so a program can get just the one power it needs
They replace passwords
They compress large files
items.explanation: Instead of running a program as full root (which is risky), capabilities grant just one specific power. If the program is compromised, the damage is limited to that single power.

items.prompt: Why is granting a program one capability safer than running it as full root?
choices: Because root is slower
Because if the program is tricked, an attacker gets only that one narrow power, not control of the whole system
Because capabilities cannot be inspected
Because root cannot read files
items.explanation: Full root means full control of the machine, so a compromised root program is a disaster. A single capability limits the blast radius to just that one ability.

items.prompt: Which command lets you inspect the capabilities granted to a program like /usr/bin/ping?
choices: getfacl /usr/bin/ping
getcap /usr/bin/ping
chmod /usr/bin/ping
ls -l /usr/bin/ping
items.explanation: getcap reads a program's capabilities. getfacl reads ACLs, chmod changes basic permissions, and ls -l shows the rwx bits but not the capabilities.

items.prompt: As a beginner, when should you reach for ACLs and capabilities?
choices: On every single file, always
Only when the basic owner/group/other model and ordinary permissions are not enough for the job
Never; they do not work
Only on the root user's files
items.explanation: The basic model handles most needs. ACLs and capabilities are extra tools for the less common cases (sharing with one extra person, or granting one narrow power) and are good to know exist.

lessons.title: What Is a Process? PIDs and the Process Tree
lessons.content:
Open the Task Manager on Windows or Activity Monitor on a Mac and you see a list of running apps. Linux has the same idea, and we have a word for each running program: a **process**. A process is simply a program that is currently running. Your web browser is a process. The little clock in the corner is a process. The computer runs many of them at the same time.

So it can tell them apart, the system gives every process a number called a **PID** (Process ID) — like a ticket number. No two running processes share a PID.

Processes don't appear from nowhere: one process starts another. The starter is called the **parent**, and the new one is its **child**. Each process remembers its parent's PID, called the **PPID** (Parent Process ID). Follow the parents upward and you get a **process tree** — a family tree of every program. At the very top sits **PID 1**, the first process the system starts when it boots (named `init`, and on most modern systems that's a program called `systemd`). Everything else descends from it.

You can see the tree with one command:

```bash
pstree
```

Don't worry about the details of how a process is created yet (Linux clones the parent and then loads the new program — you'll meet this later). For now: a process is a running program, it has a PID, it has a parent, and they all branch out from PID 1.

That mental model — running programs, each with an ID, arranged in a tree — is the foundation for listing them, inspecting them, and (next lessons) sending them messages.

items.prompt: In Linux, what is a process?
choices: A program that is currently running
A file stored on the hard drive
A folder in the home directory
A network connection
items.explanation: A process is simply a program while it is running. A program sitting on disk is just a file; it only becomes a process once it starts running. Folders and network connections are different things entirely.

items.prompt: What is a PID?
choices: The name of the program's author
A unique number that identifies a running process
The amount of memory a program uses
The folder where a program is installed
items.explanation: PID stands for Process ID — a unique number, like a ticket number, that the system gives each running process so it can tell them apart. It is not a name, a memory amount, or a location.

items.prompt: A process's PPID tells you what?
choices: How long the process has been running
The PID of the process that started it (its parent)
How much CPU the process is using
Whether the process is allowed to run
items.explanation: PPID stands for Parent Process ID — the PID of the process that started this one. Processes are started by other processes, and the PPID records which one was the parent.

items.prompt: If process A starts process B, what is the relationship between them?
choices: A is the child of B
They are unrelated
A is the parent of B
B replaces A
items.explanation: The process that starts another is the parent; the new one is the child. Since A started B, A is the parent and B is the child. They are related, and B does not replace A — both keep running.

items.prompt: What is special about PID 1?
choices: It is the fastest process on the system
It is the first process started at boot, and everything else descends from it
It is always your web browser
It uses the most memory
items.explanation: PID 1 is the very first process the system starts when it boots (usually a program called systemd). It sits at the root of the process tree, and every other process descends from it. It has nothing to do with speed or memory.

items.prompt: What does the "process tree" describe?
choices: The folders on the hard drive
The order in which programs were installed
The parent/child relationships between processes, branching out from PID 1
A list of programs sorted by name
items.explanation: The process tree shows which process started which, branching out from PID 1 at the top — like a family tree. It is not about folders, install order, or alphabetical sorting.

items.prompt: Which command shows processes arranged as a tree?
choices: pstree
mkdir
ping
echo
items.explanation: pstree draws the process tree, showing parents and their children. mkdir makes folders, ping tests network reachability, and echo prints text — none of them show processes.

items.prompt: Roughly how many processes does a typical Linux system run at once?
choices: Exactly one
Only the programs you opened yourself
Many — your programs plus lots of background system programs
None until you press a key
items.explanation: A running system has many processes at once: the apps you opened plus many background programs the system needs (logging, networking, the display, and so on). Just like Task Manager shows far more than the windows you opened.

items.prompt: Which everyday tool is most like the Linux idea of listing processes?
choices: A calculator
Windows Task Manager or macOS Activity Monitor
A web browser's bookmarks
A spell checker
items.explanation: Task Manager and Activity Monitor both show the programs currently running and let you act on them — exactly the role process tools play on Linux. The others have nothing to do with running programs.

items.prompt: In the diagram, a process whose PPID is 990 was started by which process?
choices: PID 1 (init / systemd)
The login shell (PID 842)
The web server (PID 990)
It has no parent
items.explanation: PPID is the parent's PID. A PPID of 990 means the web server (PID 990) is the parent. Every process except PID 1 has a parent, and the arrows in the tree always point from parent to child.

lessons.title: Listing and Inspecting Processes
lessons.content:
Now that you know what a process is, how do you actually see the ones running on your machine? Linux gives you a few tools, and they're a lot like opening Task Manager to glance at what's busy.

The basic one is **`ps`** (short for "process status"). On its own it shows just your current shell's processes, so people usually ask for *all* of them:

```bash
ps aux        # list every process: who owns it, its PID, CPU and memory use
```

`ps` is a one-time snapshot — a photo of that instant. For a **live, updating** view that refreshes every second or two, use **`top`** (or the friendlier, colorful **`htop`** if it's installed):

```bash
top           # live view; press q to quit
htop          # nicer version, if installed
```

Often you just want to find one program by name instead of reading a huge list. **`pgrep`** searches by name and prints matching PIDs:

```bash
pgrep firefox     # prints the PID(s) of any process named firefox
```

While looking at these tools you'll notice a **STAT** (state) column with a single letter. A process is always in one **state** — a snapshot of what it's doing right now. The common ones, in plain terms:

- **R** — *Running*: using the CPU or ready to.
- **S** — *Sleeping*: waiting for something (most idle programs sit here). It wakes when needed.
- **T** — *Stopped*: paused (you'll cause this with Ctrl-Z in a later lesson).
- **Z** — *Zombie*: it has *finished*, but its parent hasn't collected the result yet. A zombie isn't really running — think of it as a receipt waiting to be filed.
- **D** — *Uninterruptible sleep*: stuck deep in a hardware operation (like a slow disk). It's rare, and we'll come back to why it matters.

Recap: `ps aux` for a snapshot, `top`/`htop` for a live view, `pgrep` to find by name — and the STAT letter tells you whether a process is running, sleeping, stopped, or finished.

items.prompt: What does the ps command do?
choices: Lists processes that are currently running
Pauses the computer
Prints the contents of a file
Powers off the system
items.explanation: ps ("process status") lists running processes. It does not pause or power off anything, and it does not print file contents — that would be a command like cat.

items.prompt: Why do people usually type 'ps aux' rather than just 'ps'?
choices: 'aux' makes it run faster
Plain ps shows only a few of your own processes, while 'ps aux' shows every process on the system
'aux' sorts the list alphabetically
Plain ps is not a real command
items.explanation: By itself ps shows only the processes tied to your current shell. The 'aux' options ask for all processes on the system, with owner, PID, CPU and memory columns. It is not about speed or sorting.

items.prompt: How does top differ from ps?
choices: top only works as the root user
top shows a live view that keeps updating, while ps is a one-time snapshot
top can only show one process at a time
There is no difference
items.explanation: top continuously refreshes, like watching a live dashboard, while ps prints a single snapshot and exits. Both can show many processes, and top does not require root.

items.prompt: What is htop?
choices: A command to shut down the machine
A friendlier, colorful version of top for viewing live processes
A tool for editing text files
A way to delete a process permanently from disk
items.explanation: htop is an easier-to-read, colorful alternative to top showing the same kind of live process view. It is not an editor, and it does not shut down the machine.

items.prompt: You want the PID of a program called firefox without scrolling a long list. Which command helps?
choices: pgrep firefox
mkdir firefox
cat firefox
cd firefox
items.explanation: pgrep searches processes by name and prints the matching PIDs — perfect for finding one program quickly. mkdir makes a folder, cat prints a file, and cd changes directory.

items.prompt: A process shows the state Z (zombie). What does that mean?
choices: It is using 100% of the CPU
It has finished, but its parent hasn't collected its result yet
It is infected with a virus
It is the most important process on the system
items.explanation: A zombie has already finished running; it just hasn't been cleaned up yet because its parent hasn't collected its exit result. It uses almost no resources — think of it as a receipt waiting to be filed, not a busy or infected process.

items.prompt: Most idle background programs show the state S. What does S mean?
choices: Stopped permanently
Sleeping — waiting for something, and will wake when needed
Saving a file
Slow
items.explanation: S means sleeping: the process is simply waiting (for input, a timer, or a request) and will wake up when there's work to do. It is not permanently stopped, and S is not about saving or speed.

items.prompt: Which state means a process is using the CPU or ready to?
choices: R (Running)
Z (Zombie)
T (Stopped)
D (Uninterruptible sleep)
items.explanation: R means running or runnable — actively using the CPU or ready to. Z is a finished process awaiting cleanup, T is paused, and D is stuck in a hardware operation.

items.prompt: Which best describes the output of 'ps aux'?
choices: A live view that keeps changing on screen
A single snapshot of the processes at the moment you ran it
A sound alert when a process starts
A backup of all your files
items.explanation: ps prints a snapshot — a photo of that instant — then returns to the prompt. For a continuously updating view you'd use top or htop instead.

items.prompt: Looking at the state diagram, what has to happen for a zombie (Z) to fully disappear?
choices: You must restart the computer
Its parent collects its result (reaps it)
It must start running again
You must delete a file
items.explanation: A zombie has already finished; it only lingers until its parent collects its result. Once reaped, the entry is gone — no reboot, no file deletion, and it never runs again.

lessons.title: Signals: Talking to Processes
lessons.content:
Sometimes you need to tell a running program to do something — most often, to stop. Linux does this by sending the process a **signal**: a tiny pre-set message the system delivers, a bit like tapping someone on the shoulder. Each signal has a name and a number.

The two you'll use most are about stopping a program, and they're exactly like the difference between *asking* an app to close and *force-quitting* it:

- **SIGTERM** (number 15) — the polite "please stop." The program gets the message and can finish up first: save its work, tidy up, then exit. This is the default.
- **SIGKILL** (number 9) — the forceful "stop right now, no choice." The system removes the program instantly. It gets *no* chance to save or clean up.

You send signals with the **`kill`** command (an unfortunate name — it really means "send a signal"):

```bash
kill 4242        # sends SIGTERM (the polite default) to PID 4242
kill -9 4242     # sends SIGKILL (force) to PID 4242
```

A few more signals you'll meet:

- **SIGINT** (2) — what you send by pressing **Ctrl-C** in the terminal: "interrupt, stop this."
- **SIGSTOP** and **SIGCONT** — pause a process and later resume it, like a pause button.
- **SIGHUP** (1) — originally "the terminal closed"; many background programs now treat it as "reload your settings."

Most signals can be "caught" — the program can install its own handler and decide what to do (that's how SIGTERM lets a program clean up). But **SIGKILL cannot be caught or ignored.** That's on purpose: there must always be a guaranteed way to stop a misbehaving program. The cost is that SIGKILL skips all cleanup.

Recap: a signal is a small message to a process. Try **SIGTERM** first (`kill`) so the program can shut down nicely; reach for **SIGKILL** (`kill -9`) only when it won't stop — and remember SIGKILL can't be caught.

items.prompt: What is a signal in Linux?
choices: A small pre-set message the system delivers to a process
A file that a program writes to disk
A type of network cable
A folder where programs are stored
items.explanation: A signal is a tiny, pre-defined message sent to a process — like tapping it on the shoulder — most often to tell it to stop. It is not a file, cable, or folder.

items.prompt: SIGTERM vs SIGKILL — which should you try first when stopping a program, and why?
choices: SIGKILL first, because it is faster to type
SIGTERM first, because it politely asks the program to stop so it can save and clean up before exiting
It makes no difference which you use
SIGKILL first, because SIGTERM doesn't actually stop anything
items.explanation: SIGTERM is the polite request: the program receives it and can save its work and tidy up before exiting. SIGKILL forces an instant stop with no chance to clean up, so you save it for when SIGTERM doesn't work. SIGTERM does stop well-behaved programs.

items.prompt: Why can't SIGKILL be caught or ignored by a program?
choices: Because it is the newest signal
So there is always a guaranteed way to stop a misbehaving program
Because only the root user can send it
Because it is actually the same as SIGTERM
items.explanation: SIGKILL is deliberately uncatchable so there is always a last-resort way to force-stop a program, even a buggy one that ignores everything else. The trade-off is that it gets no chance to clean up. It is not new, and it differs from SIGTERM.

items.prompt: You run 'kill 4242' with no number. Which signal does that send?
choices: SIGKILL (9)
SIGTERM (15), the polite default
SIGINT (2)
No signal at all
items.explanation: With no signal specified, kill sends SIGTERM (15) by default — the polite request to stop — giving the program a chance to shut down cleanly. You'd add '-9' to send SIGKILL.

items.prompt: What does 'kill -9 4242' do?
choices: Sends SIGKILL to PID 4242, forcing it to stop immediately with no cleanup
Restarts PID 4242
Sends a polite stop request that the program can ignore
Lists nine processes
items.explanation: Signal 9 is SIGKILL, so 'kill -9 4242' forces process 4242 to stop right away with no chance to save or clean up. It does not restart anything, and SIGKILL cannot be ignored.

items.prompt: Pressing Ctrl-C in the terminal to stop a running command sends which signal?
choices: SIGINT
SIGKILL
SIGHUP
SIGCONT
items.explanation: Ctrl-C sends SIGINT (interrupt), telling the foreground program to stop. SIGKILL is the forceful kill, SIGHUP often means "reload," and SIGCONT resumes a paused process.

items.prompt: What do SIGSTOP and SIGCONT do?
choices: They delete and recreate a process
SIGSTOP pauses a process; SIGCONT resumes it later
They both stop a process permanently
They change a process's name
items.explanation: SIGSTOP is a pause button and SIGCONT is the resume button — the process is frozen, then continued where it left off. Neither deletes the process nor changes its name.

items.prompt: The command is called 'kill', but what does it really do?
choices: It always destroys files
It sends a signal to a process — which often, but not always, asks it to stop
It can only ever force-stop programs
It shuts down the whole computer
items.explanation: Despite the name, kill simply sends a signal. The default signal (SIGTERM) politely asks a process to stop, and other signals do other things like pause or reload. It does not touch files or power off the machine.

items.prompt: Many background programs treat SIGHUP as a request to do what?
choices: Use more memory
Reload their settings without fully restarting
Run faster
Change their PID
items.explanation: SIGHUP originally meant "the terminal closed," but many long-running background programs now repurpose it to mean "reload your configuration." It has nothing to do with memory, speed, or changing a PID.

items.prompt: Based on the diagram, what is the main downside of using SIGKILL instead of SIGTERM?
choices: It is slower to take effect
The program gets no chance to clean up, so unsaved work or stray temp files may be left behind
It only works on background programs
It can be ignored by the program
items.explanation: SIGKILL stops the program instantly with no cleanup, so it may leave unsaved data or leftover lock/temp files. That's why you try the polite SIGTERM first. SIGKILL is fast and cannot be ignored.

lessons.title: Job Control, Priorities & Limits
lessons.content:
When you run a command in the terminal, it normally takes over until it finishes — you wait, staring at the prompt. That's a **foreground** job. But what if a command takes ten minutes and you'd like to keep working? You send it to the **background**. Managing foreground and background work is called **job control**.

The simplest trick: add **`&`** to the end of a command to start it in the background right away.

```bash
sleep 1000 &     # runs in the background; you get the prompt back
jobs             # lists the background/stopped jobs in this terminal
```

Already started something and wish you'd backgrounded it? Press **Ctrl-Z** to *pause* it (this puts it in the Stopped state). Then choose:

```bash
bg               # resume the paused job, now in the background
fg               # bring a job back to the foreground
```

So: `Ctrl-Z` pauses, `bg` resumes it in the background, `fg` pulls it back to the front.

By default, background jobs stop when you close the terminal. To keep one running after you log out, start it with **`nohup`**, or use **`disown`** on a job that's already running:

```bash
nohup ./backup.sh &    # keeps running after you log out
disown               # detach the most recent job from this terminal
```

Two more ideas, kept light:

**Priority (`nice`).** Every process has a *niceness* value from -20 to 19. Higher means "nicer" — it yields the CPU to others, so higher number = lower priority. Start a low-priority job with `nice`, and change a running one with `renice`. Only the administrator can give a process *higher* priority (a negative value).

```bash
nice -n 15 ./big-job.sh    # run it at low priority
```

**Resource limits (`ulimit`).** A process has ceilings on things like how many files it can open at once. View them with `ulimit -a`. You rarely change these as a beginner — just know the cap exists.

Recap: `&` backgrounds, `Ctrl-Z` pauses, `fg`/`bg` move jobs around, `nohup`/`disown` outlive the terminal; `nice` sets politeness and `ulimit` shows the limits.

items.prompt: What is a foreground job?
choices: A command that takes over the terminal until it finishes
A command that always runs as the administrator
A command stored on the desktop
A command that never stops
items.explanation: A foreground job holds the terminal: you wait for it before you can type the next command. Backgrounding lets you keep working instead. It has nothing to do with admin rights or the desktop.

items.prompt: What does adding '&' to the end of a command do?
choices: Deletes the command's output
Runs the command in the background so you get the prompt back right away
Runs the command twice
Forces the command to stop
items.explanation: A trailing '&' starts the command in the background and returns the prompt immediately, so you can keep working while it runs. It does not duplicate, delete output, or stop anything.

items.prompt: You're running a command in the foreground and press Ctrl-Z. What happens?
choices: The command is deleted from disk
The command is paused (stopped), and you get the prompt back
The command speeds up
The terminal closes
items.explanation: Ctrl-Z pauses the foreground job, putting it in the Stopped state and returning your prompt. You can then resume it with fg or bg. It does not delete the program or close the terminal.

items.prompt: After pausing a job with Ctrl-Z, which command resumes it running in the background?
choices: fg
bg
jobs
nice
items.explanation: bg resumes the paused job in the background. fg brings it back to the foreground, jobs just lists jobs, and nice is about priority, not resuming.

items.prompt: What does the 'jobs' command show?
choices: Every process on the whole system
The background and paused jobs started in your current terminal
A list of available jobs to apply for
The files in the current folder
items.explanation: jobs lists the background and stopped jobs belonging to your current terminal session. To see every process system-wide you'd use ps or top instead.

items.prompt: Why might you start a command with 'nohup'?
choices: To make it run as administrator
So it keeps running even after you close the terminal or log out
To delete its output
To pause it
items.explanation: nohup lets a command survive after you log out or the terminal closes — useful for long jobs. It does not grant admin rights, delete output, or pause anything.

items.prompt: What is 'disown' used for?
choices: Detaching an already-running job so it isn't tied to the terminal anymore
Renaming a process
Giving a process more memory
Forcing a process to stop
items.explanation: disown detaches a job that's already running so it won't be stopped when the terminal closes — similar in goal to nohup but applied after the fact. It doesn't rename, resize, or stop the process.

items.prompt: A process has a high niceness value (for example, 15). What does that mean?
choices: It has high priority and grabs the CPU first
It is 'nicer' — lower priority, yielding the CPU to other processes
It uses no memory
It cannot be stopped
items.explanation: Higher niceness means lower priority: the process is being 'nice' and lets others use the CPU first. A common point of confusion — higher number means less priority, not more.

items.prompt: What does 'ulimit' deal with?
choices: The system's clock
Per-process resource ceilings, such as how many files a process may open at once
The order processes start at boot
Which user owns a file
items.explanation: ulimit shows and sets per-process resource limits, like the maximum number of open files. It is unrelated to the clock, boot order, or file ownership.

items.prompt: Who can give a process a higher priority (a negative niceness)?
choices: Any user at any time
Only the administrator (root)
No one — priority can never change
Only the process itself
items.explanation: Raising priority (a negative niceness) is reserved for the administrator. Ordinary users may only make a process nicer (lower priority). Priority can change — just not upward for non-admins.

items.prompt: In the foreground vs background diagram, which command moves a background job back to the foreground?
choices: fg
bg
nohup
items.explanation: fg brings a job to the foreground. bg sends it (back) to the background, '&' starts a command in the background, and nohup keeps a job alive after logout.

lessons.title: Who Are You? Users and the root Superuser
lessons.content:
Imagine a single computer shared by a whole family. Each person has their own login so their files, settings, and email stay separate. Linux works exactly like that, all the time. Linux is **multi-user**: more than one account can exist (and even be logged in) on the same machine.

A **user** is simply an account. Behind the scenes every user has a number called a **UID** (user ID, a unique number the system uses to tell accounts apart). You see a friendly name like `alice`, but the computer really thinks in numbers. The name is just a label for humans.
