Not all users are people. Many users are **background services** — programs like a web server or a printer service that run quietly and need an account of their own. So "user" means "any account," person or program.

One account is special: **root**, also called the **superuser**. Root can do *anything* — read any file, change any setting, delete anything. That is a lot of power, so on a healthy system you do **not** log in as root for daily work. You log in as a normal user and only borrow root's power for the rare moment you truly need it (you'll meet `sudo` for that later). Think "Run as administrator" on Windows: you click it only when needed.

To see who you currently are:

```bash
whoami        # prints your user name, e.g. alice
id            # shows your UID and the groups you belong to
id -u         # just your numeric UID
```

Logging in (typing your password) is how you **prove who you are**. That proof is called **authentication** — the computer checking that you really are the person you claim to be, like showing a keycard at a door.

**Recap:** Linux is multi-user; every account is a user with a numeric UID; root (the superuser) can do anything, so you stay a normal user and borrow root power only when needed; and logging in proves your identity (authentication).

items.prompt: What does it mean that Linux is "multi-user"?
choices: Only one person can ever use the computer
More than one account can exist on the same machine, each kept separate
It can only run on servers, not laptops
Every program must be written by multiple people
items.explanation: Multi-user means many accounts (people and background services) can exist and be kept separate on one machine, like separate logins on a shared family computer. It has nothing to do with how programs are written or what hardware it runs on.

items.prompt: What is a UID?
choices: A unique number the system uses to identify a user account
The user's password
The name of the user's home folder
A type of network cable
items.explanation: A UID (user ID) is the unique number Linux really uses to tell accounts apart. The friendly name like 'alice' is just a label for humans; it is not the password or the home folder.

items.prompt: Who, or what, is the 'root' account?
choices: A locked account no one can ever use
The superuser account that can do anything on the system
The account for guests with the fewest powers
The folder at the top of the filesystem
items.explanation: root is the superuser: it can read, change, or delete anything. (The very top folder '/' is also called root, but that is a directory, not the user account here.) Guests have the fewest powers, the opposite of root.

items.prompt: Why is it best to log in as a normal user instead of as root for everyday work?
choices: Root accounts are slower
Root cannot open a web browser
Root can do anything, so one mistake could damage the whole system
Normal users get more storage space
items.explanation: Because root has unlimited power, a single typo or bad command could wipe out the system. Staying a normal user limits the damage from mistakes. Speed, browsers, and storage are not the reason.

items.prompt: Are all users on a Linux system people?
choices: Yes, every user is always a human being
No, many users are background services (programs) with their own accounts
No, users are only ever programs, never people
Yes, but only one person is allowed per computer
items.explanation: A 'user' is any account. Besides people, background programs like a web server get their own user accounts. So users can be people or services.

items.prompt: Which command prints just the name of the user you are currently logged in as?
choices: whoami
passwd
reboot
mkdir
items.explanation: whoami prints your current user name. passwd changes a password, reboot restarts the machine, and mkdir makes a folder — none of those tell you who you are.

items.prompt: When you type your password to log in, the system checks that you are who you claim to be. What is this checking called?
choices: Compilation
Authentication
Compression
Encryption
items.explanation: Authentication is proving your identity — like showing a keycard at a door. Compilation turns source code into a program, compression shrinks data, and encryption scrambles data; none of those mean 'prove who you are'.

items.prompt: The command 'id' is run and shows 'uid=1000(alice)'. What does the number 1000 represent?
choices: alice's password
How many times alice has logged in
alice's numeric user ID (UID)
The amount of memory alice is using
items.explanation: 1000 is alice's UID — the number the system uses internally for her account. The name 'alice' in parentheses is the human label for that same number. It is not a password, a login count, or memory usage.

items.prompt: A good everyday comparison for the root/superuser idea is:
choices: A guest Wi-Fi password
The 'Run as administrator' option you use only when needed
Turning the screen brightness up
Renaming a file
items.explanation: Like 'Run as administrator' on Windows, root power is used only when truly needed. Guest Wi-Fi, brightness, and renaming files are ordinary low-risk actions, not all-powerful administrator actions.

lessons.title: Groups: Sharing Access Between Users
lessons.content:
Suppose alice and bob both work on the family photos and need to open and edit the same files. You could grant each person access one by one, but that gets messy fast. Linux has a tidier way: **groups**.

A **group** is a named bucket of users. You put alice and bob into a group called `photos`, give the photo files to that group, and now both members can use them. Add a third person to the group later and they get access automatically — no need to touch each file.

Just like users have a UID, every group has a number called a **GID** (group ID). And just like usernames, the group name is the friendly label while the GID is what the system tracks underneath.

A user can belong to several groups at once. There are two kinds of membership, but the idea is simple: you have one **primary group** (your main group, used by default for new files you create) and you can be an extra member of many other **supplementary groups** (the additional buckets you've been added to). To see your groups:

```bash
groups        # lists the groups you belong to
id            # also shows your groups, with their GID numbers
```

Groups are also how Linux hands out special privileges. For example, members of a group often named `sudo` (or `wheel`) are allowed to borrow root powers. Being added to that group is how a normal user gets permission to run admin tasks.

One important gotcha: when someone adds you to a new group, your **current** login session does not see the change yet. Your group membership is locked in at the moment you log in. You must **log out and back in** (start a fresh session) for the new group to take effect. This surprises everyone the first time.

**Recap:** A group is a named bucket of users (with a numeric GID) used to share access; you have one primary group plus any number of supplementary ones; some groups grant privileges like sudo; and a new group membership only applies after you start a new login session.

items.prompt: What is a group in Linux?
choices: A named collection of users that can share access
A backup copy of all your files
A type of password
A folder that cannot be deleted
items.explanation: A group is a named bucket of users, used so several people can share access to the same files or privileges. It is not a backup, a password, or a special folder.

items.prompt: alice and bob both need to edit the same photo files. What is the cleanest way to arrange that?
choices: Give them both the root password
Put them in a shared group and give the files to that group
Email the files back and forth
Make both of them UID 0
items.explanation: A shared group is exactly the tidy tool for this: both members get access, and adding a third person later is automatic. Sharing the root password or setting UID 0 hands out unlimited power, which is dangerous and overkill.

items.prompt: What is a GID?
choices: The name of a file
The numeric ID of a group
A graphics setting
A user's login password
items.explanation: A GID (group ID) is the number that identifies a group, just as a UID identifies a user. It is not a filename, a graphics setting, or a password.

items.prompt: Can one user belong to more than one group at the same time?
choices: No, a user can only ever be in one group
Yes — one primary group plus any number of supplementary groups
Only root can be in more than one group
Only if the user has no password
items.explanation: A user has one primary group (used by default for new files) and can be a supplementary member of many other groups. This is normal for every user, not just root, and has nothing to do with passwords.

items.prompt: Which command lists the groups you currently belong to?
choices: groups
delete
format
shutdown
items.explanation: The 'groups' command lists your group memberships (and 'id' shows them with GID numbers). 'delete', 'format', and 'shutdown' do not report your groups.

items.prompt: An administrator just added carol to the 'sudo' group, but in her already-open terminal she still cannot run admin tasks. Why?
choices: The change needs a reboot of the whole building's power
Group changes only apply after she starts a new login session
Her keyboard is broken
Groups never affect what a user can do
items.explanation: Group membership is locked in when a session starts. Carol must log out and back in (a fresh session) for the new group to take effect. A reboot is not required — just a new login — and groups absolutely do affect what a user can do.

items.prompt: What is the difference between a primary group and a supplementary group?
choices: There is no difference; the words mean the same thing
The primary group is your main/default group; supplementary groups are extra ones you also belong to
Supplementary groups are only for root
The primary group is your password and the supplementary group is your username
items.explanation: You have exactly one primary (default) group plus any number of supplementary (extra) groups. They are different roles, both available to ordinary users, and neither is a password or username.

items.prompt: Why are groups often used to hand out privileges like the ability to use sudo?
choices: Because adding a user to the right group is a simple, central way to grant the same permission to many people
Because groups make the computer run faster
Because every user must be in the sudo group
Because groups delete files automatically
items.explanation: Putting a user in a group like 'sudo' is a clean, central way to grant a permission, and it scales to many people. Groups do not speed up the machine, are not mandatory for every user, and do not delete files.

lessons.title: Creating Accounts and the Files Behind Them
lessons.content:
Now that you know what users and groups are, how do you make and manage them? And where does Linux actually keep this information?

Linux stores accounts in a couple of plain text files. You normally don't edit them by hand — commands do it for you — but it helps to know they exist.

**`/etc/passwd`** lists the accounts. Despite the name, it holds **no passwords**. Each line describes one user, with fields separated by colons:

```
alice:x:1000:1000:Alice Smith:/home/alice:/bin/bash
```

That reads as: user name `alice`; an `x` standing in for the password; UID `1000`; primary group GID `1000`; a comment (full name); the home folder; and the **login shell** (the program you get when you log in). This file is readable by everyone, which is why it must not contain the real password.

**`/etc/shadow`** is where the real secret lives — but not the password itself. It stores a **password hash**: a scrambled, one-way version of your password. You can turn a password into its hash, but you cannot turn the hash back into the password. When you log in, Linux hashes what you typed and compares it to the stored hash. `/etc/shadow` is readable by **root only**, so other users cannot copy your hash and try to crack it. (Common misconception: the password is *not* kept in `/etc/passwd`.)

**`/etc/group`** lists the groups and who belongs to them.

Useful commands (admin actions need `sudo`, which you'll meet next):

```bash
sudo useradd -m alice     # create user alice, -m makes her home folder
sudo passwd alice         # set or change alice's password
sudo usermod -aG photos alice   # add alice to the 'photos' group (-aG = add to group)
groups alice              # show which groups alice is in
id alice                  # show alice's UID, primary GID, and groups
who                       # show who is logged in right now
```

A tip you saw before: a group added with `usermod` only takes effect after a fresh login.

**Recap:** `/etc/passwd` lists accounts (no passwords) in colon fields; `/etc/shadow` holds the one-way password hash and is root-only; `/etc/group` lists groups; and `useradd`, `passwd`, `usermod`, `id`, `groups`, and `who` create and inspect accounts.

items.prompt: Despite its name, what does the file /etc/passwd actually contain?
choices: Everyone's actual passwords in plain text
A list of accounts and their details — but no passwords
Only the root password
A list of installed programs
items.explanation: /etc/passwd lists accounts (name, UID, primary group, home, login shell) but holds no passwords — just an 'x' placeholder. It is readable by everyone, which is exactly why real passwords are kept out of it.

items.prompt: Where are password hashes stored, and why not in /etc/passwd?
choices: In /etc/passwd, because it is convenient
In /etc/shadow, which only root can read, so others cannot copy the hashes
In the user's home folder, in a file called password.txt
Nowhere — Linux does not store anything about passwords
items.explanation: Hashes live in /etc/shadow, which is readable by root only — so ordinary users cannot grab everyone's hashes and try to crack them. /etc/passwd is world-readable, so putting secrets there would be unsafe. Linux definitely does store a hash; it just keeps it protected.

items.prompt: What is a password 'hash'?
choices: The password typed backwards
A scrambled, one-way version of the password that cannot be turned back
A nickname for the password
The number of characters in the password
items.explanation: A hash is a one-way scramble: you can turn a password into its hash but not the hash back into the password. At login, Linux hashes what you typed and compares. It is not the password reversed, a nickname, or a length count.

items.prompt: In the line 'alice:x:1000:1000:Alice Smith:/home/alice:/bin/bash', what does the last field '/bin/bash' represent?
choices: Alice's password
Alice's login shell — the program she gets when she logs in
Alice's email address
The amount of disk space alice may use
items.explanation: The final field is the login shell, the program started when the user logs in (here, the bash shell). It is not a password, email, or disk quota.

items.prompt: Which command creates a new user named alice along with her home folder?
choices: sudo useradd -m alice
sudo rm alice
sudo passwd -d alice
sudo reboot alice
items.explanation: useradd creates the account and the -m flag makes her home folder. 'rm' deletes, 'passwd -d' removes a password, and 'reboot' restarts the machine — none create a user.

items.prompt: Which command sets or changes alice's password?
choices: sudo passwd alice
sudo useradd alice
groups alice
who alice
items.explanation: passwd sets or changes a password (writing the hash into /etc/shadow). useradd creates an account, 'groups' lists group membership, and 'who' shows who is logged in.

items.prompt: What does 'sudo usermod -aG photos alice' do?
choices: Deletes the photos group
Adds alice to the 'photos' group
Changes alice's password to 'photos'
Renames alice to photos
items.explanation: usermod -aG adds the user to a group (the -aG means 'add to this group'). It does not delete the group, change a password, or rename the user. Remember alice must start a new login session for it to take effect.

items.prompt: Which file lists the groups on the system and who belongs to them?
choices: /etc/group
/etc/shadow
/etc/hostname
/etc/photos
items.explanation: /etc/group lists groups and their members. /etc/shadow holds password hashes, /etc/hostname holds the computer's name, and /etc/photos is not a standard file.

items.prompt: Why do you normally use commands like useradd instead of opening /etc/passwd and /etc/shadow in a text editor?
choices: The files are encrypted and cannot be opened
The commands fill in the fields correctly and keep the files consistent, avoiding mistakes
Editing the files is illegal
The files do not really exist
items.explanation: The commands fill in fields in the right format and keep related files in sync, which avoids easy hand-editing mistakes that could lock you out. The files are plain text (not encrypted), they do exist, and editing them is allowed — just risky.

lessons.title: Borrowing Root Power: sudo vs su
lessons.content:
You know root can do anything, and that you should stay a normal user. So how do you do the occasional task that *needs* root power — like installing software? You **borrow** root's power briefly. There are two classic tools: `sudo` and `su`.

**`sudo`** means "do this one command as the superuser." You type `sudo` in front of a command, enter **your own** password, and just that command runs with root power:

```bash
sudo apt update        # update the software list, as root, just this once
```

When the command finishes, you are an ordinary user again. Two nice things: you use **your** password (no need to know root's), and the system can **record** who ran what, so there's a trail.

You earn the right to use `sudo` by being in a group such as `sudo` or `wheel`, or by a rule in a settings file called `/etc/sudoers` (you'll meet it later). The key idea now: `sudo` borrows power for **one command at a time**.

**`su`** means "switch user." By itself, `su` tries to *become* root, asking for **root's** password, and then leaves you sitting in a root shell where everything you type runs as root until you exit. `su alice` switches to alice instead.

Why prefer `sudo`?
- `sudo` needs *your* password; `su` (to root) needs the *root* password, which ideally no human knows or shares.
- `sudo` runs **one** command then drops back; `su` leaves you in an all-powerful shell where it's easy to forget you're root and make a costly mistake.
- `sudo` actions are logged per command.

This is also why logging in directly as root is discouraged: it's like staying in "administrator mode" all day. Borrowing power for a single command is far safer.

**Recap:** `sudo` runs one command as root using your own password and is logged; `su` switches into another user's shell (root by default) using *that* user's password and keeps that power until you exit; prefer `sudo` because it's per-command, safer, and uses your own credentials.

items.prompt: What does 'sudo apt update' do?
choices: Logs you in permanently as root
Runs just that one command (apt update) with root power, then returns you to normal
Deletes the root account
Changes your password to 'apt'
items.explanation: sudo runs a single command with root power and then you are an ordinary user again. It does not log you in as root permanently, delete root, or change your password.

items.prompt: When you run a command with sudo, whose password do you normally type?
choices: Root's password
Your own password
No password is ever needed
Another random user's password
items.explanation: sudo asks for YOUR own password and checks that you are allowed to use sudo. That means no one needs to know or share root's password. (su, by contrast, asks for the target account's password.)

items.prompt: Why is using sudo generally preferred over logging in as root and working there all day?
choices: sudo makes commands run faster
sudo borrows root power for just one command and records who ran it, limiting risk
Logging in as root is impossible
sudo gives you more disk space
items.explanation: sudo grants root power per command and keeps a log, so a mistake affects only that one command and there's a trail of who did what. Staying logged in as root is like leaving administrator mode on all day — one slip can be disastrous. Speed and disk space are not the reason.

items.prompt: What does the 'su' command (with no name after it) try to do?
choices: Shut the computer down
Switch you into a root shell, asking for root's password
Show the system uptime
Update all software
items.explanation: 'su' means 'switch user', and by itself it switches to root, prompting for ROOT's password, leaving you in a root shell. It does not shut down, show uptime, or update software.

items.prompt: A key difference: after 'su' to root succeeds, what is your situation compared with running a single 'sudo' command?
choices: You are dropped back to a normal user immediately, same as sudo
You stay in a root shell, where everything you type runs as root until you exit
Nothing changes; su has no effect
Your files are deleted
items.explanation: su leaves you in an ongoing root shell — every command runs as root until you type 'exit'. sudo, by contrast, runs just one command then returns you to normal. su does have an effect and does not delete files.

items.prompt: How does a normal user typically earn the right to use sudo?
choices: By being added to a group such as 'sudo' or 'wheel', or by a rule in /etc/sudoers
By guessing the root password
Everyone can use sudo automatically
By renaming their home folder
items.explanation: Permission to use sudo comes from membership in a group like 'sudo'/'wheel' or a rule in /etc/sudoers. It does not require the root password, is not granted to everyone by default, and has nothing to do with folder names.

items.prompt: One safety advantage of sudo is that the system can keep a record of sudo commands. Why does that matter?
choices: It makes the disk fill up faster
It provides a trail of who ran which admin command, useful for accountability
It speeds up the network
It hides what administrators do
items.explanation: Logging sudo commands creates an audit trail showing who did what — helpful for security and troubleshooting. It is about accountability, not disk usage or speed, and the point is to reveal admin actions, not hide them.

items.prompt: Which statement about the root password is the safest practice on a well-run system?
choices: Everyone should memorize the root password
No human needs to know root's password; people use sudo with their own passwords instead
The root password should be the same as every user's password
Print the root password and tape it to the monitor
items.explanation: Best practice is that no one needs the root password day to day — authorized users borrow root power via sudo with their own passwords. Sharing, reusing, or posting the root password are all serious security risks.

lessons.title: Behind the Scenes: Service Accounts, sudoers, Keys and Login
lessons.content:
This lesson zooms out to a few ideas you'll bump into later. The goal is recognition, not mastery — a one-line mental model for each.

**Service accounts and nologin shells.** Remember that many users are background programs, not people. A program like a web server gets its own **service account** so it runs with only the access it needs (this is called *least privilege* — give each account the minimum power required). Such accounts usually get a special login shell called **nologin**: instead of giving a usable shell, it just prints a short message and exits. So even if someone got hold of that account, they couldn't open a working session with it.

**`/etc/sudoers` and `visudo`.** Earlier you used `sudo`. The rules for *who* may run *which* commands as root live in a file called `/etc/sudoers`. A rule can be broad ("alice may run anything") or precise ("the deploy account may run *only* the restart-web command"). It can also say **NOPASSWD** — meaning that specific command runs via sudo without asking for a password, handy for automated scripts. Because a typo here could lock everyone out of sudo, you edit it with a special tool, `visudo`, which checks the file for mistakes before saving.

**SSH key-based login.** When connecting to another computer over the network, you often skip typing a password entirely. Instead you use a **key pair**: a private key you keep secret and a matching public key placed on the server. The key proves who you are — more convenient and stronger than a password. (You'll cover SSH in depth in the networking topic.)

**PAM and nsswitch (one line each).** **PAM** (Pluggable Authentication Modules) is the framework programs use to check logins, so each program doesn't reinvent password checking. **nsswitch** (the Name Service Switch, configured in `/etc/nsswitch.conf`) tells the system *where* to look up accounts — local files, or a central company directory. In big organizations, accounts live in such a directory so one login works on every machine, but the overall login journey is the same.

The diagram shows that journey: a login program collects your username and secret, the system verifies it, looks up your account, sets your UID/GID and groups, and finally starts your shell.

**Recap:** Service accounts run programs with least privilege and often use a nologin shell; `/etc/sudoers` (edited safely with `visudo`) defines who may run which commands as root, optionally NOPASSWD; SSH key login uses a key pair instead of a password; and PAM checks logins while nsswitch decides where accounts are looked up.

items.prompt: What is a 'service account'?
choices: An account a background program runs under, with only the access it needs
A paid subscription to Linux
The account used by the customer-service department
Another name for the root account
items.explanation: A service account belongs to a background program (like a web server) and is given only the access it needs — least privilege. It is not a subscription, a department's login, or root.

items.prompt: What does giving a service account a 'nologin' shell accomplish?
choices: It makes the account log in faster
It means the account cannot open a usable interactive session — the shell just prints a message and exits
It gives the account root power
It deletes the account
items.explanation: A nologin shell prints a short message and exits instead of opening a working shell, so even a stolen service credential can't be used for an interactive session. It does not speed up login, grant root, or delete the account.

items.prompt: The idea of giving each account only the power it actually needs is called:
choices: Maximum privilege
Least privilege
Open access
Root by default
items.explanation: Least privilege means granting only the minimum access required, which limits damage if an account is misused. The other options describe handing out too much power, the opposite of the goal.

items.prompt: What does the file /etc/sudoers control?
choices: The desktop wallpaper
Who may run which commands as root via sudo, and whether a password is required
Which websites can be visited
How much memory each program uses
items.explanation: /etc/sudoers defines the sudo rules: which users may run which commands as root, and options like NOPASSWD. It has nothing to do with wallpaper, web filtering, or memory limits.

items.prompt: Why edit /etc/sudoers with the special tool 'visudo' rather than any text editor?
choices: visudo is prettier
visudo checks the file for mistakes before saving, so a typo can't lock everyone out of sudo
Only visudo can open the file at all
visudo automatically gives everyone root
items.explanation: visudo validates the syntax before saving; a broken sudoers file could otherwise lock everyone (including you) out of sudo. It is about safety, not looks, and it does not grant everyone root.

items.prompt: A sudoers rule says the 'deploy' account may run ONLY the command that restarts the web service, with NOPASSWD. What can 'deploy' do?
choices: Run any command as root without a password
Restart the web service as root without being asked for a password — and nothing else via sudo
Log in as root interactively
Edit any file on the system
items.explanation: The rule grants exactly one command, as root, with no password prompt (handy for automation). It does not grant other commands, root login, or general file editing. Scoping NOPASSWD to one specific command keeps it relatively safe.

items.prompt: In SSH key-based login, what proves your identity instead of a typed password?
choices: A key pair — a secret private key you hold plus a matching public key on the server
Your phone number
The computer's serial number
A typed password, always
items.explanation: SSH key login uses a key pair: you keep the private key secret, the server holds the matching public key, and the key proves who you are — more convenient and stronger than a password. It does not rely on a phone or serial number, and the whole point is to avoid typing a password.

items.prompt: In one line, what is PAM (Pluggable Authentication Modules)?
choices: A program that draws the login screen
The framework programs use to check logins, so each program doesn't reinvent authentication
A type of computer memory
A backup tool
items.explanation: PAM is the shared authentication framework that applications call to verify logins, so they don't each implement password checking. It is not the login screen's graphics, RAM, or a backup tool.

items.prompt: In one line, what does nsswitch (the Name Service Switch, /etc/nsswitch.conf) decide?
choices: Which network cable to use
WHERE the system looks up accounts and groups — e.g. local files or a central company directory
The speed of the login screen
How passwords are scrambled
items.explanation: nsswitch tells the system where to find account and group information — local files and/or a central directory. It does not pick cables, set screen speed, or define password hashing.

items.prompt: Put the login journey in the right order.
choices: Start the shell first, then ask for the username last
Collect username and secret, verify the secret, look up the account, set UID/GID and groups, then start the login shell
Delete the account, then log in
Set the wallpaper, then verify the password
items.explanation: The login program collects your username and secret, the system verifies it, looks up your account, sets your UID/GID and group memberships, and finally starts your login shell. The other orderings are nonsensical — you can't run the shell before knowing who you are.

subtopics.title: The Boot Chain: Firmware to Init

lessons.title: The Big Picture: From Power Button to Login
lessons.content:
Have you ever wondered what happens in the few seconds between pressing the power button and seeing a login screen? The computer doesn't wake up all at once. It comes alive in **stages**, and each stage's only job is to start the next one. This sequence is called the **boot chain** ("to boot" comes from "bootstrap" — the machine pulling itself up by its own bootstraps).

Think of a relay race. Each runner carries the baton a short distance, then hands it to the next runner. No single runner does the whole race. The boot chain works the same way:

1. **Firmware** — a small program built into the computer's motherboard. It checks the hardware is working, then finds a disk to start from.
2. **Bootloader** — a program on that disk (the common one is called GRUB). Its job is to load the operating system's core, the kernel.
3. **Kernel** — the heart of Linux. It takes control of the hardware and gets the system ready.
4. **init** — the very first regular program. Today this is almost always a program called **systemd**. It starts all the other services (networking, sound, the desktop) until you reach a login prompt.

Why learn this? Because when a computer won't boot, the screen usually freezes at *one* of these stages. Knowing the chain tells you which runner dropped the baton — and that is the first step to fixing it.

**Recap:** power on → firmware → bootloader → kernel → init → login. Five handoffs, in that order. Everything else in this sub-topic just zooms in on one stage at a time.

items.prompt: Put the boot stages in the correct order, from pressing the power button to reaching a login prompt.
choices: Power on → kernel → firmware → bootloader → init → login
Power on → firmware → bootloader → kernel → init → login
Power on → bootloader → firmware → init → kernel → login
Power on → init → kernel → bootloader → firmware → login
items.explanation: The order is firmware first (it is built into the hardware and runs before anything on disk), then the bootloader it finds on disk, which loads the kernel, which finally starts init. Remembering this order is the single most useful thing in this sub-topic.

items.prompt: What is the main idea behind the term "boot chain"?
choices: The computer does everything at once the moment you press power
The computer wakes up in stages, and each stage's job is to start the next one
The chain refers to physically chaining several disks together
It is the cable that connects the power button to the motherboard
items.explanation: Booting happens in stages that hand off one to the next, like a relay race passing a baton. Nothing happens "all at once," and the word has nothing to do with cables or chaining disks.

items.prompt: Which stage runs first, before anything stored on the hard disk gets a chance to run?
choices: The kernel
The bootloader
The firmware
init (systemd)
items.explanation: Firmware is built into the motherboard itself, so it is the only thing available the instant power arrives. The bootloader, kernel, and init all live on a disk and cannot run until the firmware finds that disk.

items.prompt: What is the bootloader's main job in the chain?
choices: To test that the memory and hardware are working
To load the operating system's kernel into memory and start it
To show you the login prompt
To start the networking and sound services
items.explanation: The bootloader (commonly GRUB) sits between the firmware and the kernel. Hardware testing is the firmware's job; the login prompt and starting services come later, after init takes over.

items.prompt: On a typical modern Linux system, which program is started as the very first regular program (init)?
choices: GRUB
systemd
The firmware
The login screen
items.explanation: Today init is almost always systemd. GRUB is the bootloader (an earlier stage), the firmware runs even earlier, and the login screen only appears after init has started the needed services.

items.prompt: Why is understanding the boot chain useful when a computer refuses to start?
choices: It lets you skip the boot process entirely
The screen usually freezes at one particular stage, and knowing the stages tells you where the failure is
It makes the computer boot faster automatically
It is only useful for designing new computers, not fixing them
items.explanation: A boot failure happens at a specific stage. If you know the chain, the point where it freezes tells you which "runner dropped the baton," which is the first step toward a fix. It does not speed anything up by itself.

items.prompt: After the kernel finishes its early setup, what does init (systemd) do?
choices: It tests the computer's hardware
It loads the kernel from disk
It starts the other services until you reach a login prompt
It shuts the computer back down
items.explanation: init is the parent of all other programs; it starts services such as networking and the desktop, ending at a login prompt. Testing hardware is the firmware's job, and loading the kernel is the bootloader's.

items.prompt: The relay-race analogy describes the boot chain as a series of handoffs. What is being "handed off" at each step?
choices: A physical baton inside the computer
Control of the machine, passed from one stage to the next
Electrical power, which each stage uses up
The user's password
items.explanation: Each stage does a small job and then hands control to the next stage. There is no real baton; it is a way to picture how responsibility passes along the chain. Power and passwords are not what gets passed.

items.prompt: Which statement about the kernel is correct, based on the big picture?
choices: The kernel runs before the firmware
The kernel is loaded by the bootloader and takes control of the hardware
The kernel is the same thing as the login screen
The kernel is built into the motherboard
items.explanation: The bootloader loads the kernel, which then runs the hardware as the heart of Linux. The firmware runs earlier, the kernel is not the login screen, and only the firmware is built into the motherboard.

items.prompt: Look at the diagram of the boot chain. Reading it left to right, which sequence does it show?
choices: Login → init → kernel → bootloader → firmware → power
Power button → firmware → bootloader → kernel+initramfs → init (systemd) → login
Firmware → power button → kernel → login → init
Power button → kernel → firmware → init → bootloader → login
items.explanation: The diagram reads left to right as a relay of handoffs: power, then firmware, then the bootloader, then the kernel (with its initramfs), then init/systemd, ending at the login prompt — the same order to memorize.

lessons.title: Firmware: UEFI and Old BIOS
lessons.content:
When you press power, the computer needs *some* program to run — but nothing useful is in memory yet, because the operating system lives on a disk that hasn't been read. The fix: a small program is permanently stored in a chip on the motherboard. That program is the **firmware**. "Firmware" just means software baked into hardware so it's always there.

The firmware has two jobs. First, a quick hardware check often called **POST** (Power-On Self-Test): is there memory, a keyboard, a disk? Second, **find a boot device** — a disk that contains something to start — and launch the bootloader on it.

There are two kinds of firmware:

**UEFI** (Unified Extensible Firmware Interface) is the modern standard, on essentially every computer made in the last decade. UEFI understands files and folders. It looks on the disk for a special area called the **EFI System Partition** (ESP) — a small storage area formatted in the simple FAT32 format — and runs a bootloader file stored there, such as `grubx64.efi`.

**BIOS** (Basic Input/Output System) is the old way, from before UEFI. Instead of reading a file, the BIOS read the raw first chunk of the disk (the very first 512 bytes) and ran whatever code was there. It works, but it's clumsy and limited. You'll meet it mostly on older machines.

The key difference: UEFI reads a real *file* from a known partition; old BIOS read raw *bytes* from the start of the disk. Either way, the result is the same — the bootloader now runs.

**Recap:** firmware is built into the motherboard, checks the hardware, finds a boot disk, and launches the bootloader. UEFI (modern) does this by reading a file from the EFI System Partition; BIOS (old) did it by reading the first raw sector.

items.prompt: What does the word "firmware" mean in this context?
choices: A program you download and install after buying the computer
Software permanently stored in a chip on the motherboard, so it is always available
A type of hard disk
The login screen of the operating system
items.explanation: Firmware is software "baked into" hardware — it lives in a motherboard chip and is there the instant power arrives. That is exactly why it can run before any disk is read.

items.prompt: What are the two main jobs of the firmware when you power on?
choices: Show the desktop and load your documents
Check the hardware (POST), then find a boot device and launch its bootloader
Encrypt the disk and connect to Wi-Fi
Start systemd and reach the login prompt
items.explanation: Firmware does a Power-On Self-Test to verify the hardware, then locates a disk to boot from and hands off to its bootloader. Desktops, logins, and systemd all come much later in the chain.

items.prompt: What does POST (Power-On Self-Test) do?
choices: It posts a message to a remote server
It quickly checks that basic hardware like memory and disks are present and working
It loads the kernel into memory
It logs the user in automatically
items.explanation: POST is the firmware's quick hardware self-check at power-on. It is unrelated to networking, and loading the kernel or logging in happens in later stages.

items.prompt: Which firmware type is the modern standard found on essentially all recent computers?
choices: BIOS
UEFI
GRUB
POST
items.explanation: UEFI is today's standard. BIOS is the older approach it replaced, GRUB is a bootloader (a later stage), and POST is the hardware self-test, not a firmware type.

items.prompt: What is the EFI System Partition (ESP)?
choices: The partition that holds all your personal documents
A small FAT32-formatted area on the disk where UEFI finds and runs bootloader files
A chip on the motherboard that stores the firmware
The part of memory the kernel runs in
items.explanation: The ESP is a small, simply-formatted (FAT32) partition that UEFI reads to find a bootloader file like grubx64.efi. It is on the disk, not a motherboard chip, and it does not store your documents.

items.prompt: What is the key difference in HOW UEFI versus old BIOS launches the bootloader?
choices: UEFI reads a real file from the EFI System Partition; BIOS read raw bytes from the disk's first 512-byte sector
UEFI uses the keyboard; BIOS uses the mouse
UEFI runs after the kernel; BIOS runs before the firmware
There is no difference; the names are interchangeable
items.explanation: UEFI understands files and reads a bootloader file from the ESP. Old BIOS instead executed the raw code in the disk's very first sector. The end result is the same — the bootloader starts — but the method differs.

items.prompt: Why does the firmware need to run before the bootloader, kernel, or init?
choices: Because the bootloader refuses to start without the firmware's permission code
Because at power-on nothing useful is in memory yet, and only the firmware (in a motherboard chip) is already there to run
Because the firmware is faster than the other stages
Because the kernel is too large to load first
items.explanation: Right at power-on the disk has not been read, so memory holds nothing useful. The firmware is the one program physically present in a chip, so it must run first and find the disk for everything else.

items.prompt: What format is the EFI System Partition typically stored in, and why does that matter?
choices: FAT32, a simple format the firmware can read without needing complex drivers
An encrypted format, so no one can read the bootloader
The same format as the rest of Linux, so the firmware needs no special handling
A raw, unformatted area with no filesystem at all
items.explanation: The ESP uses FAT32, a deliberately simple and universal format that UEFI firmware can read easily. A more complex or encrypted filesystem would be harder for the lightweight firmware to handle this early.

items.prompt: After the firmware finishes its work, what happens next in the boot chain?
choices: The login prompt appears immediately
The bootloader it found and launched begins running
The computer powers off
The kernel starts init directly, skipping the bootloader
items.explanation: The firmware's last act is to launch the bootloader, so the bootloader runs next. The kernel, init, and login all come afterward, in that order.

items.prompt: In the diagram, the UEFI firmware looks at the disk and reads one partition first before anything else. Which one?
choices: The main partition holding the root filesystem
The EFI System Partition (ESP), which holds the bootloader file
A hidden recovery partition
It reads the whole disk at once
items.explanation: UEFI reads the small FAT32 EFI System Partition first to find the bootloader (for example grubx64.efi). The larger main/root partition is used later, after the kernel has started.

lessons.title: The Bootloader: GRUB Loads the Kernel
lessons.content:
The firmware found a disk and launched a program on it. That program is the **bootloader**, and on most Linux desktops and servers it is **GRUB** (GRand Unified Bootloader). The bootloader is the bridge between firmware and the operating system.

Why do we even need a separate bootloader? The firmware is simple and limited — it just knows how to find and start one file. The bootloader is smarter: it knows where the operating system's **kernel** is stored, how to load it into memory, and how to start it. Think of GRUB as a receptionist who greets you at the door and walks you to the right room.

GRUB does three things:

1. **Shows a menu** (sometimes only briefly). If a computer has more than one operating system, or several kernel versions, GRUB lets you pick. Often it just auto-selects the default after a moment.
2. **Loads the kernel** — it reads the kernel file from the disk into memory.
3. **Passes along instructions** — GRUB hands the kernel a short line of text called the **kernel command line**: settings the kernel reads as it starts, such as which disk holds the main system. (More on that next lesson.)

You usually don't write GRUB's menu by hand. It's generated from configuration files, and a command regenerates the menu when you change something — but as a beginner you mostly just need to recognize GRUB's menu when you see it at boot.

A quick contrast: small embedded gadgets (think a router or a single-board computer with an ARM chip) often use a different bootloader called **U-Boot** instead of GRUB. Same idea — load the kernel — just a different tool for tiny hardware.

**Recap:** the bootloader (GRUB on most PCs) loads the kernel into memory, optionally lets you choose which one, and passes it a kernel command line. It exists because the firmware is too simple to do this itself.

items.prompt: What is the bootloader's central job?
choices: To test the hardware at power-on
To load the operating system's kernel into memory and start it
To display the desktop wallpaper
To format the EFI System Partition
items.explanation: The bootloader loads and launches the kernel. Hardware testing is the firmware's job, and desktops appear far later, after init has done its work.

items.prompt: What does GRUB stand for, and what kind of program is it?
choices: A firmware chip on the motherboard
The GRand Unified Bootloader, the common bootloader on Linux PCs and servers
A part of the Linux kernel
A type of disk partition
items.explanation: GRUB is the GRand Unified Bootloader — the bootloader stage that runs after the firmware. It is not firmware, not part of the kernel, and not a partition.

items.prompt: Why is a separate bootloader needed instead of letting the firmware load the kernel directly?
choices: The firmware is forbidden by law from loading a kernel
The firmware is simple and only knows how to start one file; the bootloader is smarter about finding and loading the kernel
The kernel can only be loaded by another kernel
The bootloader is faster than the firmware at testing memory
items.explanation: Firmware is intentionally minimal — it just launches one file. The bootloader adds the smarts to locate the kernel, load it, and pass it settings. It is a division of labor, not a legal rule.

items.prompt: What is the "kernel command line" that GRUB passes to the kernel?
choices: A line of text with startup settings the kernel reads, such as which disk holds the main system
The command you type to log in
A list of all the files on the disk
The serial number of the motherboard
items.explanation: The kernel command line is a short string of startup options the kernel reads as it boots. It is set by GRUB, not typed by the user at login, and it is not a file list or a serial number.

items.prompt: Why does GRUB sometimes show a menu during boot?
choices: To let you type your password
To let you choose between multiple operating systems or kernel versions if more than one is installed
To run the Power-On Self-Test
To format the disk before booting
items.explanation: The GRUB menu exists so you can pick which OS or kernel to start when several are available; otherwise it usually auto-selects a default. Passwords, POST, and formatting are not what the menu is for.

items.prompt: Which bootloader is commonly used on small embedded devices with ARM chips, instead of GRUB?
choices: systemd
U-Boot
POST
FAT32
items.explanation: U-Boot is the bootloader often used on tiny ARM-based hardware like routers and single-board computers. systemd is init (a much later stage), POST is a hardware test, and FAT32 is a disk format.

items.prompt: Where does the bootloader sit in the boot chain?
choices: Before the firmware
Between the firmware and the kernel
After init (systemd)
At the very end, right before login
items.explanation: The firmware launches the bootloader, and the bootloader loads the kernel — so it sits squarely between the two. It does not run before the firmware or after init.

items.prompt: As a beginner, what is the most important thing to recognize about GRUB's menu?
choices: You must hand-write the whole menu file before every boot
It is the screen where you can pick which operating system or kernel to start
It is the same as the login screen
It only appears after the kernel has fully loaded
items.explanation: Recognizing GRUB's menu — the boot-time screen for choosing an OS or kernel — is the practical takeaway. The menu is generated from config (not hand-written each boot), it is not the login screen, and it appears before the kernel loads, not after.

items.prompt: Using the receptionist analogy, GRUB "walks you to the right room." What does the "right room" represent?
choices: The login prompt
The correct kernel to load and start
The motherboard firmware
The Wi-Fi network
items.explanation: The analogy means GRUB directs the boot to the correct kernel and loads it. The login prompt comes much later, the firmware ran earlier, and networking is unrelated to GRUB's core job.

lessons.title: The Kernel Starts — and Why initramfs Exists
lessons.content:
GRUB has loaded the **kernel** — the core of Linux — into memory and started it. The kernel now takes charge of the hardware: it sets up memory, detects devices, and prepares to run programs. Its big goal at this point is to mount the **root filesystem**: the main disk where all the system's files live, the one that becomes "`/`".

But there's a chicken-and-egg problem. To read a disk, the kernel needs the right **driver** — a small piece of code that knows how to talk to that specific kind of hardware. Modern disks come in many flavors: fast NVMe drives, encrypted volumes, RAID setups. The kernel can't include every possible driver built-in (it would be huge), so the driver it needs to reach the real disk might not be loaded yet. The kernel can't mount a disk it can't talk to.

The clever solution is the **initramfs** (initial RAM filesystem). Along with the kernel, GRUB also loaded a second small file: a tiny, temporary filesystem that lives entirely in memory. The initramfs contains just enough drivers and tools to find and open the real disk.

Think of it like a spare key kept under the doormat. You can't get into the house without the key, and the key isn't *inside* the house — so you keep a small one outside, just enough to open the front door. Once you're in, you don't need it anymore.

The sequence: the kernel mounts the initramfs first, runs the drivers inside it, uses them to find and mount the real root disk, then **switches over** to that real disk as "`/`". The initramfs is then thrown away, freeing the memory.

**Recap:** the kernel needs to mount the real disk, but might lack the driver to reach it. The initramfs is a tiny temporary in-memory filesystem carrying those drivers — a stepping-stone that gets the kernel to the real root, then disappears.

items.prompt: What is the kernel's main goal once GRUB has started it?
choices: To show the GRUB menu
To take charge of the hardware and mount the real root filesystem (the main disk, "/")
To run the Power-On Self-Test
To pick which operating system to boot
items.explanation: After starting, the kernel controls the hardware and works toward mounting the root filesystem so the system's files become available. The GRUB menu, POST, and OS selection all happened in earlier stages.

items.prompt: What is the initramfs FOR? (the key idea of this lesson)
choices: It is a permanent backup copy of all your files
It is a tiny temporary in-memory filesystem holding the drivers the kernel needs to find and mount the real disk
It is the firmware stored on the motherboard
It is the login screen shown before the desktop
items.explanation: The initramfs is a small, temporary RAM-based filesystem that carries just enough drivers and tools to reach the real root disk. It is not a backup, not firmware, and not the login screen.

items.prompt: What is a "driver" in this context?
choices: A person who maintains the computer
A small piece of code that knows how to talk to a specific kind of hardware, like a particular disk
Another name for the kernel command line
The cable connecting the disk to the motherboard
items.explanation: A driver is software that lets the kernel communicate with a specific device. Without the right driver, the kernel cannot read that disk — which is exactly the problem the initramfs solves.

items.prompt: Why might the kernel be unable to mount the real disk on its own, right away?
choices: Because the disk is always encrypted by law
Because the kernel can't build in every possible driver, so the driver for that exact disk type may not be loaded yet
Because GRUB deletes the disk during boot
Because the firmware locks the disk until login
items.explanation: Including every driver would make the kernel enormous, so the needed driver for a particular NVMe, encrypted, or RAID disk may not be present initially. The kernel cannot mount hardware it cannot yet talk to.

items.prompt: In the "spare key under the doormat" analogy, what does the spare key represent?
choices: Your login password
The initramfs — just enough to open the door (mount the real disk), kept outside the house
The firmware in the motherboard chip
The GRUB menu
items.explanation: The spare key is the initramfs: a small thing kept outside the "house" (the real disk) that holds just enough to open the front door. Once inside, it is no longer needed — just like the initramfs is discarded after the real root is mounted.

items.prompt: Which describes the correct sequence the kernel follows with the initramfs?
choices: Mount the real disk, then load the initramfs, then delete the kernel
Mount the initramfs first, use its drivers to find and mount the real root disk, then switch to the real disk and discard the initramfs
Run the initramfs as the permanent root forever
Skip the initramfs and ask the firmware to mount the disk
items.explanation: The kernel mounts the temporary initramfs, runs its drivers to reach the real root, switches over to that real disk, and then frees the initramfs. It is a stepping-stone, never the permanent root.

items.prompt: What happens to the initramfs after the real root filesystem is mounted?
choices: It stays in memory permanently as the main filesystem
It is thrown away, freeing the memory it used
It is copied onto the disk for next time
It becomes the kernel
items.explanation: Once the real root is mounted, the initramfs has done its job and is discarded so its memory can be reused. It is temporary by design and does not become the kernel or get saved to disk.

items.prompt: Where does the initramfs live while it is in use?
choices: On the EFI System Partition
Entirely in memory (RAM)
Inside the motherboard firmware chip
On the real root disk it is trying to mount
items.explanation: The initramfs is an "initial RAM filesystem" — it lives in memory. It cannot live on the real root disk, since its whole purpose is to reach that disk in the first place.

items.prompt: What does it mean that the kernel "mounts" the root filesystem?
choices: It physically screws the disk into the case
It makes the disk's files available to the system, attached at the path "/"
It deletes everything on the disk
It copies the disk into the firmware
items.explanation: Mounting means making a filesystem's contents accessible, attached at a location in the directory tree — here, at the root "/". It has nothing to do with physically installing the drive or erasing it.

items.prompt: Two files are loaded together by GRUB at boot. What are they?
choices: The firmware and the BIOS
The kernel and the initramfs
The login screen and the desktop
The EFI System Partition and the root disk
items.explanation: GRUB loads both the kernel and its initramfs into memory. The firmware/BIOS run before GRUB, and the login screen and desktop come much later via init.

items.prompt: The diagram shows three steps. Which step lets the kernel finally reach the real disk?
choices: Step 1, where the kernel starts but cannot yet read the real disk
Step 2, where the initramfs supplies the drivers needed to reach the disk
There is no step that reaches the disk
Step 1, because the kernel never needs any drivers
items.explanation: In step 1 the kernel lacks the driver; step 2 is the initramfs providing that driver so the kernel can mount the real root in step 3. The initramfs is the bridge between not-reachable and reachable.

lessons.title: The Handoff to init and systemd
lessons.content:
The kernel has mounted the real disk. Now comes a special moment: the kernel starts the very first ordinary program. That program becomes **PID 1** — process number 1, the first process — and it is called **init**. On modern Linux, init is a program named **systemd**.

This is the boundary between two worlds. Before this moment, the kernel was running everything itself ("kernel space"). After it, normal programs run on top of the kernel ("user space"). The kernel doesn't stop — it keeps running underneath, managing hardware — but from now on it starts programs *as requested* rather than driving the boot itself.

PID 1 is special in two ways. It is the **ancestor of every other program**: everything else is started by it, or by something it started. And it **never exits** while the computer is on — if PID 1 stopped, the system would have no one in charge.

What does systemd actually do? It starts all the services that make a usable system: networking, logging, the sound system, the graphical desktop, and finally the **login prompt**. It starts many of them **in parallel** (at the same time) where it safely can, which is part of why modern systems boot quickly.

A bit of history: before systemd, the classic init was **SysV init**, which started services strictly one after another using numbered scripts and "runlevels" (numbered system states). systemd replaced runlevels with **targets** — named goals like `multi-user.target` (a full text-mode system) or `graphical.target` (a full desktop). You don't need to memorize these as a beginner; just know that a "target" is systemd's word for "the state we're booting toward."

**Recap:** the kernel starts init (systemd) as PID 1 — the first process, ancestor of all others, which never exits. systemd then starts the system's services, often in parallel, ending at the login prompt. This is the final handoff in the boot chain.

items.prompt: What is PID 1?
choices: The first hard disk in the computer
Process number 1 — the very first ordinary program the kernel starts, called init
The first partition on the disk
The firmware's identification number
items.explanation: PID 1 is the first process: the init program the kernel launches once the real disk is mounted. It is not a disk, partition, or firmware ID.

items.prompt: On a modern Linux system, which program is init (PID 1)?
choices: GRUB
systemd
The kernel
UEFI
items.explanation: Today systemd is init. GRUB is the bootloader, the kernel is a separate (earlier) thing that starts init, and UEFI is firmware from the very start of the chain.

items.prompt: Why is the start of PID 1 described as a "boundary"?
choices: It is where the disk is physically partitioned
It marks the switch from the kernel driving the boot (kernel space) to normal programs running on top of it (user space)
It is the boundary between two different disks
It is where the firmware hands off to GRUB
items.explanation: Starting PID 1 is the crossing point from kernel space (the kernel running the show) to user space (ordinary programs). The firmware-to-GRUB handoff happened much earlier in the chain.

items.prompt: What makes PID 1 special compared to ordinary programs?
choices: It runs only for one second and then quits
It is the ancestor of every other program and never exits while the computer is on
It runs inside the firmware chip
It is the only program that can read the disk
items.explanation: PID 1 starts everything else (directly or indirectly) and must keep running the whole time the system is up. If it exited, nothing would be in charge. It runs in user space, not in the firmware.

items.prompt: Does the kernel stop running once init (systemd) starts?
choices: Yes, the kernel shuts down and systemd takes its place entirely
No, the kernel keeps running underneath, managing hardware, while programs run on top of it
Yes, but only until login
No, but the kernel is copied into the initramfs
items.explanation: The kernel never stops while the system runs; it continues managing hardware beneath user-space programs. init does not replace the kernel — it runs on top of it.

items.prompt: What does systemd do after it becomes PID 1?
choices: It tests the memory and finds a boot disk
It starts the system's services (networking, logging, desktop) and finally the login prompt
It loads the kernel into memory
It runs the GRUB menu
items.explanation: systemd's job as init is to bring up all the services that make a usable system, ending at the login prompt. Memory testing/boot-disk finding is the firmware's job, and loading the kernel is GRUB's.

items.prompt: One reason modern systems boot quickly is that systemd does what?
choices: Skips starting most services
Starts many services in parallel (at the same time) where it safely can
Runs entirely inside the firmware
Boots without a kernel
items.explanation: systemd starts independent services in parallel rather than strictly one-by-one, which speeds up boot. It does not skip needed services, and it certainly still requires the kernel.

items.prompt: How did the older SysV init differ from systemd?
choices: SysV started services strictly one after another using numbered scripts and runlevels
SysV ran before the firmware
SysV was a type of disk
SysV did not need a kernel
items.explanation: The classic SysV init started services serially (one at a time) via numbered scripts and runlevels. systemd replaced that with parallel startup and named targets. Both run as init, after the kernel.

items.prompt: In systemd, what is a "target" (such as graphical.target)?
choices: A named goal describing the state the system is booting toward, like a full desktop
A physical disk to boot from
A driver inside the initramfs
A setting in the motherboard firmware
items.explanation: A target is systemd's word for a state to reach, such as graphical.target (full desktop) or multi-user.target (full text-mode). It replaced the older numbered runlevels and has nothing to do with disks or firmware.

items.prompt: Putting it together: which is the correct final stretch of the boot chain?
choices: Kernel mounts the real disk → starts init (systemd) as PID 1 → systemd starts services → login prompt
systemd starts the kernel → kernel finds the disk → login → PID 1
Login prompt → kernel → systemd → disk mounted
Kernel → firmware → systemd → GRUB → login
items.explanation: After mounting the real root, the kernel starts systemd as PID 1, which brings up services and ends at the login prompt. The kernel starts systemd — not the other way around — and firmware/GRUB ran long before this stage.

items.prompt: The diagram divides the picture into "kernel space" and "user space." What is the single arrow between them?
choices: The firmware testing the hardware
The handoff where the kernel starts init (PID 1, systemd) in user space
GRUB loading the kernel
The login prompt appearing
items.explanation: The arrow is the handoff: the kernel (kernel space) starts init as PID 1 in user space. GRUB loading the kernel and the firmware's hardware test were earlier stages, and login comes after this handoff.

lessons.title: When Boot Goes Wrong: Diagnostics
lessons.content:
Now the payoff. You understand the chain: firmware → bootloader → kernel → initramfs → init → login. When a machine misbehaves — boots slowly, or a service fails — Linux keeps detailed records of what happened, and a few commands let you read them. You don't need to memorize every option; just know which tool answers which question.

**`dmesg`** shows the **kernel's messages** — the running log the kernel keeps from the moment it started: which devices it found, which drivers it loaded, any hardware complaints. If a disk or network card isn't working, `dmesg` often shows the kernel struggling with it. ("dmesg" stands for "display message," referring to the kernel's message buffer.)

**`journalctl -b`** shows the **logs from the current boot**. systemd keeps a unified journal of everything that happened since power-on — every service starting, succeeding, or failing. The `-b` part means "this boot only." This is where you look when a *service* (not the hardware) failed to start. To see kernel-only messages from this boot, you can add `-k`.

**`systemd-analyze`** answers "**why was boot slow?**" On its own it prints how long each phase took (firmware, loader, kernel, userspace). Adding `blame` lists the slowest services, so you can see what dragged boot down.

The mental model: a hardware/driver problem? Reach for `dmesg`. A service that won't start? Reach for `journalctl -b`. A slow boot? Reach for `systemd-analyze`. These three cover most boot troubleshooting, and they all build on the same chain you've learned — each tool reports on a different stage of it.

**Recap:** `dmesg` for kernel/hardware messages, `journalctl -b` for this-boot service logs (`-k` for kernel-only), and `systemd-analyze` (with `blame`) for boot timing. Match the tool to the question, and the boot chain becomes something you can debug, not just describe.

items.prompt: Which command shows the kernel's own messages, such as which devices and drivers it found?
choices: dmesg
systemd-analyze blame
ls /boot
efibootmgr
items.explanation: dmesg displays the kernel's message buffer — device detection, driver loading, and hardware warnings. systemd-analyze is about boot timing, and the others are unrelated to reading kernel messages.

items.prompt: You suspect a hardware or driver problem during boot — a disk or network card that won't come up. Which tool should you reach for first?
choices: systemd-analyze
dmesg
The GRUB menu
POST
items.explanation: Hardware and driver issues show up in the kernel's messages, which dmesg displays. systemd-analyze is for timing, the GRUB menu is for choosing a kernel, and POST is the firmware's early self-test.

items.prompt: What does `journalctl -b` show?
choices: Only the firmware's settings
The logs from the current boot — systemd's unified record of what happened since power-on
A list of files in /boot
The contents of the EFI System Partition
items.explanation: journalctl reads systemd's journal, and -b limits it to the current boot: every service starting, succeeding, or failing since power-on. It is not a firmware viewer or a file lister.

items.prompt: A particular service failed to start during boot. Which command is best for investigating that?
choices: dmesg
journalctl -b
systemd-analyze
efibootmgr
items.explanation: Service start/fail records live in systemd's journal, so journalctl -b is the right tool. dmesg focuses on the kernel/hardware, and systemd-analyze is about how long boot took, not why a service failed.

items.prompt: Your computer boots, but slowly. Which command answers "why was boot slow?"
choices: dmesg
journalctl -k
systemd-analyze (especially with 'blame')
ls /
items.explanation: systemd-analyze reports how long each boot phase took, and systemd-analyze blame lists the slowest services. dmesg and journalctl -k show kernel messages, which are about correctness, not timing.

items.prompt: What does the `-b` in `journalctl -b` mean?
choices: Show messages in the background
Limit the output to the current boot only
Show only the bootloader's messages
Sort the output alphabetically
items.explanation: -b narrows the journal to the current boot, so you see only what happened since the last power-on. It is not about backgrounding, the bootloader specifically, or sorting.

items.prompt: How would you see kernel-only messages from the current boot using journalctl?
choices: journalctl -b -k
journalctl --firmware
journalctl --grub
journalctl -b --slow
items.explanation: Adding -k to journalctl -b filters to kernel messages from this boot — similar to dmesg but as a journal query. The other options are not real options.

items.prompt: Match the tool to the question: "Which services took the longest to start?"
choices: dmesg
systemd-analyze blame
journalctl -k
efibootmgr
items.explanation: systemd-analyze blame lists services ordered by how long they took to start, directly answering this question. dmesg and journalctl -k are about kernel messages, not service timing.

items.prompt: Why is knowing the boot chain helpful for using these diagnostic tools?
choices: It isn't — the tools work without any understanding
Each tool reports on a different stage of the chain, so knowing the stages tells you which tool to use
The tools only run if you can recite the chain out loud
The chain replaces the need for any diagnostic tools
items.explanation: Because each tool covers a different part of the chain (kernel/hardware, services, timing), knowing the chain points you to the right tool for a given symptom. Understanding does not replace the tools, but it makes them far more useful.

items.prompt: Pick the correct tool-to-question matching for all three tools.
choices: dmesg = slow boot; journalctl -b = hardware; systemd-analyze = service failed
dmesg = kernel/hardware messages; journalctl -b = this-boot service logs; systemd-analyze = boot timing
dmesg = login problems; journalctl -b = firmware settings; systemd-analyze = disk formatting
All three do exactly the same thing
items.explanation: dmesg reports kernel/hardware messages, journalctl -b shows this boot's service logs, and systemd-analyze reports boot timing. Matching the symptom to the right tool is the practical skill of this lesson.

lessons.title: What systemd is: the manager of background programs
lessons.content:
## The program that runs your other programs

You already met systemd in the boot chain: it is the **first program** the Linux kernel starts (process number 1), and it stays running the whole time the machine is on. But what does it actually *do* all day?

**Think of systemd as a building manager.** Every morning the manager unlocks the building and opens each shop in the right order. If a shop's lights suddenly go out, the manager notices and reopens it. systemd does the same for the **background programs** on your computer.

Those background programs have a name: a **service**, also called a **daemon** (pronounced "demon"). A service is just a program that runs quietly in the background instead of in a window you look at — things like the web server, the part that lets you log in over the network (`sshd`), or your printing system. They have no buttons; they wait and do their job.

So systemd's whole job is to:

- **Start** services when the machine boots, in the correct order.
- **Stop** and **restart** them when asked.
- **Watch** them, and **restart** one automatically if it crashes.
- **Collect their logs** in one place so you can see what happened.

You don't talk to systemd directly. You use one command, **`systemctl`** ("system control"), to tell it what to do, and another, **`journalctl`**, to read the logs.

**Recap:** systemd is PID 1, the always-on manager that starts, stops, supervises, and restarts the background programs (services / daemons) on a modern Linux system.

items.prompt: In plain terms, what is systemd's main job on a Linux system?
choices: To start, stop, supervise, and restart the background programs (services) on the machine
To edit text files for you
To draw the desktop and windows on screen
To connect the computer to Wi-Fi networks only
items.explanation: systemd is the system/service manager: it brings services up at boot, lets you control them, and restarts crashed ones. Drawing windows and editing files are jobs of other programs; networking is handled by services that systemd may manage, but that is not its whole job.

items.prompt: What is a "service" (also called a "daemon")?
choices: A program that runs quietly in the background with no window, waiting to do its job
A physical button on the computer case
A type of cable that connects to the network
The user account you log in with
items.explanation: A service/daemon is a background program (a web server, ssh, printing, etc.) that has no window and keeps running to provide something. "Daemon" is just the traditional Unix word for it; it is not hardware or an account.

items.prompt: From the boot-chain lesson: which process is systemd, and when does it start?
choices: It is process number 1 (PID 1), the first program the kernel starts, and it keeps running while the machine is on
It is the last program to start, just before shutdown
It only starts when you open a terminal
It runs inside the web browser
items.explanation: The kernel hands off to systemd as PID 1, the first userspace program, and systemd stays alive the entire session. It is not tied to terminals or browsers, and it starts at the very beginning, not the end.

items.prompt: The lesson compares systemd to a building manager. What does that analogy capture best?
choices: It opens each shop in the right order each morning and reopens any that close unexpectedly
It charges rent to each program
It decides what color the building is painted
It only works on weekends
items.explanation: The point of the analogy is ordering at startup plus automatic recovery: open things in the correct order, and reopen (restart) anything that goes down. The other options are not part of the analogy.

items.prompt: Which command do you use to TELL systemd to start, stop, or check a service?
choices: systemctl
journalctl
notepad
ifconfig
items.explanation: systemctl ("system control") is the command you use to control services. journalctl is for reading logs, not controlling services; the others are unrelated.

items.prompt: One of systemd's jobs is supervision. What does that mean in practice?
choices: It watches running services and can restart one automatically if it crashes
It asks you for permission before every action
It deletes any program it does not recognize
It runs each service exactly once and never again
items.explanation: Supervision means systemd keeps an eye on services and can bring a crashed one back up automatically. It does not delete unknown programs, and it can run a service many times (including restarts).

items.prompt: Where do the messages (logs) from services end up by default on a systemd machine?
choices: In one central log called the journal, which you read with journalctl
In a popup window for each message
They are thrown away immediately and cannot be read
Printed on paper by the printer service
items.explanation: systemd collects service output into a central journal that you read with journalctl. Logs are kept (at least for the current boot), not discarded, and certainly not printed.

items.prompt: Why is it useful that ONE manager (systemd) handles all services instead of each service fending for itself?
choices: You get one consistent way to start/stop/check every service, ordering between them, automatic restarts, and one place for logs
It makes the computer use less electricity
It removes the need to ever update software
It guarantees no program can ever crash
items.explanation: A single manager gives you uniform control, dependency ordering, automatic recovery, and centralized logging. It does not stop crashes from happening (it just reacts to them), nor does it affect power use or updates.

items.prompt: Long ago, the old init system started services by running shell scripts strictly one after another. What is one beginner-level improvement systemd brought?
choices: It can start independent services at the same time and restart them if they crash, instead of just running scripts in a fixed line
It made shell scripts impossible to write
It removed the idea of background programs entirely
It requires every service to have a graphical window
items.explanation: systemd replaced the old strictly-sequential script approach with parallel startup, ordering by stated needs, and ongoing supervision (restart on crash). Scripts still exist; services are still background programs without windows.

lessons.title: Driving services with systemctl (start, stop, enable)
lessons.content:
## The everyday commands

Now that you know systemd manages services, here is how you actually drive it. You use one command, **`systemctl`**, followed by a verb (what to do) and the service name. Service names usually end in `.service`.

Start with the safest verb — just *looking*:

```bash
systemctl status sshd.service
```

This shows whether the service is running, whether it's set to start at boot, and the last few log lines. It changes nothing.

The core control verbs:

```bash
sudo systemctl start sshd     # run it NOW
sudo systemctl stop sshd      # stop it NOW
sudo systemctl restart sshd   # stop then start (apply changes)
```

(`sudo` is needed because changing system services requires admin rights. You can usually drop the `.service` ending.)

**The one distinction beginners must get right: `enable` vs `start`.**

- **`start`** runs the service **right now**, this one time. Reboot, and it won't come back on its own.
- **`enable`** marks the service to **start automatically at every boot**. By itself, `enable` does *not* start it now.

```bash
sudo systemctl enable sshd        # auto-start at boot (not now)
sudo systemctl disable sshd       # stop auto-starting at boot
sudo systemctl enable --now sshd  # do BOTH: enable at boot AND start now
```

Think of a shop: **`start`** is unlocking the door today; **`enable`** is putting it on the manager's "open this every morning" list. Most of the time you want both, which is why `enable --now` exists.

**Recap:** `status` to look, `start`/`stop`/`restart` to control it now, `enable`/`disable` to decide whether it comes up at boot. enable = at boot; start = now.

items.prompt: You want to make a service run RIGHT NOW, just this once. Which verb?
choices: systemctl start
systemctl enable
systemctl disable
systemctl mask
items.explanation: start runs the service immediately. enable only schedules it for future boots, disable removes that scheduling, and mask blocks it entirely — none of those start it now.

items.prompt: Which command makes a service start automatically every time the computer boots — the one that makes it run at boot?
choices: systemctl enable
systemctl start
